title: EXPOSÉ XVII UNIPOTENT ALGEBRAIC GROUPS. EXTENSIONS BETWEEN UNIPOTENT GROUPS AND GROUPS OF MULTIPLICATIVE TYPE

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XVII
UNIPOTENT ALGEBRAIC GROUPS. EXTENSIONS BETWEEN UNIPOTENT GROUPS AND GROUPS OF MULTIPLICATIVE TYPEby M. Raynaud0∗

footnotes.html: cf. note on page 1 of exposé XV.

blocks.html: 0. Some notation

In the present chapter, we shall mostly have to consider algebraic groups defined over a field \(k\). The number \(p \geqslant 0\) will always denote the characteristic of \(k\), \(\mathbb{F}_p\) the prime field with \(p\) elements if \(p > 0\), \(\bar{k}\) an algebraically closed extension of \(k\), and \(q\) a prime number distinct from \(p\).

For every S-prescheme, \((\mathbb{G}_a)_\mathrm{S}\) (resp. \((\mathbb{G}_m)_\mathrm{S}\)) denotes the additive group (resp. the multiplicative group) over S (cf. Exp. I 4.3). For every integer \(n > 0\), \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)_\mathrm{S}\) (resp. \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_\mathrm{S}\)) denotes the group of \(n^{\text{th}}\) roots of unity (Exp. I 4.4.4) (resp. the constant group over S associated with the abstract group \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) (Exp. I 4.1)). The group \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_\mathrm{S}\) is finite and étale over S; the group \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)_\mathrm{S}\) is flat and finite over S, and is étale over S if and only if \(n\) is invertible on S (Exp. VIII 2.1).

If S is a prescheme of characteristic \(p > 0\), for every integer \(n > 0\) and every S-group prescheme G, we denote by \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G})\) the radicial sub-S-group prescheme of G equal to the kernel of the \(n^{\text{th}}\) iterate of the Frobenius morphism relative to G (Exp. VIIA). In particular, if \(\mathrm{G} = (\mathbb{G}_a)_\mathrm{S}\) we set \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G}) = (\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^n})_\mathrm{S}\), which is a radicial S-group, flat and finite over S, which represents the following functor: for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\), \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^n})_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}')\) is the set of \(x' \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'})\) such that \(x'^{p^n} = 0\).
The group \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_{p^n})_\mathrm{S}\), already defined, is canonically isomorphic to \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathbb{G}_m)_\mathrm{S}\).

When there is no ambiguity about the base scheme S, we shall simply write \(\mathbb{G}_a\), \(\mathbb{G}_m\), \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^n}\), etc. instead of \((\mathbb{G}_a)_\mathrm{S}\), \((\mathbb{G}_m)_\mathrm{S}\), \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^n})_\mathrm{S}\), etc.
If G is a commutative S-group prescheme, for every integer \(n > 0\), \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) is the sub-group prescheme of G equal to the kernel of raising to the \(n^{\text{th}}\) power in G.

For the convenience of the reader, we have collected in an appendix some properties of algebraic groups proved in Exp. VI and VII, as well as elementary properties of Hochschild cohomology, which will be useful to us in this chapter.
1. Definition of unipotent algebraic groups

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

An algebraic group G defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\) is said to be unipotent if G admits a composition series whose successive quotients are isomorphic to algebraic subgroups of \((\mathbb{G}_a)_k\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, K an algebraically closed extension of \(k\), and G an algebraic group defined over \(k\). Then, for G to be unipotent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}\) be unipotent.
The necessity of the condition is clear, since a composition series gives a composition series by extension of the base.

The proof of sufficiency is standard: K is the inductive limit of its sub-\(k\)-algebras of finite type. By Exp. VIB §10, we can find a sub-\(k\)-algebra A of K of finite type, a composition series \(\mathrm{G}_i\) of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}\) (\(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{A}\)) and immersions \(u_i : \mathrm{H}_i = \mathrm{G}_i/\mathrm{G}_{i+1} \to (\mathbb{G}_a)_\mathrm{S}\). To prove that G is unipotent, it then suffices to make a \(k\)-extension of the base: \(\mathrm{A} \to k\), which is possible, since \(\operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-alg}}(\mathrm{A}, k)\) is not empty, A being a nonzero \(k\)-algebra of finite type over the algebraically closed field \(k\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3

Let G be an algebraic group defined over a field \(k\). We shall say that G is unipotent if there exists an algebraically closed extension \(\bar{k}\) of \(k\) such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) is unipotent (definition 1.1).
By 1.2, this property is independent of the chosen algebraically closed extension \(\bar{k}\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.4

Let G be an algebraic group defined over a field \(k\) and H an algebraic group defined over an extension \(k'\) of \(k\). We shall say that H is a form of G over \(k'\) if the algebraic groups \(\mathrm{G}_{k'}\) and \(\mathrm{H}_{k'}\) become isomorphic over \(\bar{k'}\) (as above, one sees that this property does not depend on the choice of the algebraically closed extension \(\bar{k'}\) of \(k'\)). We shall also say that H is a "twisted" group G.
We are now in a position to describe the algebraic subgroups of \(\mathbb{G}_a\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p \geqslant 0\). Then an algebraic subgroup H of \((\mathbb{G}_a)_k\) is of one of the following types:

(i) \(\mathrm{H} = 0\).

(ii) \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_a\).

(iii) (If \(p > 0\)) H is an extension of a twisted constant group \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^r\) by a radicial group \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^n}\) (\(r\) and \(n\) integers \(\geqslant 0\), \(r + n > 0\)). If moreover \(k\) is perfect, this extension is necessarily trivial.

Proof. If H is of dimension 1, it is clear that \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_a\). Otherwise H is of dimension 0 and consequently is an extension1 of an étale group \(\mathrm{H}''\) by its identity component \(\mathrm{H}'\), which is a radicial group. To describe \(\mathrm{H}''_{\bar{k}}\), it suffices to know the abstract group \(\mathrm{H}''(\bar{k})\), isomorphic to \(\mathrm{H}(\bar{k})\). Now the latter is a finite subgroup of \(\mathbb{G}_a(\bar{k})\), hence is zero if \(p = 0\) and of the form \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^r\) otherwise, since it is killed by \(p\). The group \(\mathrm{H}'\) is closed in \(\mathbb{G}_a\) and is defined by a single equation (the ring \(k[\mathrm{T}]\) of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) is a principal ideal ring), which over \(\bar{k}\) admits 0 as its only root, so this equation is of the form \(\mathrm{T}^n = 0\). Compatibility with the group law implies that \((\mathrm{T} + \mathrm{T}')^n\) belongs to the ideal generated by \(\mathrm{T}^n\) and \(\mathrm{T}'^n\) in the ring \(k[\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{T}']\); hence: if \(p = 0\), we have \(n = 1\) and \(\mathrm{H}'\) is the unit group; if \(p > 0\), we have \(n = p^m\) and \(\mathrm{H}' = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^m}\).
The last assertion of 1.5 follows more generally from the lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6

If \(k\) is a perfect field, every extension H of an étale algebraic group \(\mathrm{H}''\) by a radicial group \(\mathrm{H}'\) is trivial. Moreover there exists a unique lifting of \(\mathrm{H}''\) into H, namely \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}}\).

Indeed, \(k\) being perfect, the reduced \(k\)-scheme \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}}\) is an algebraic subgroup of H (Exp. VIA 0.2) which is geometrically reduced, hence smooth over \(k\) (Exp. VIA 1.3.1), hence étale and of dimension 0. To see that the canonical projection \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}} \to \mathrm{H}''\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to check it after extension of the base field \(k \to \bar{k}\), in which case it suffices to show that we have an isomorphism on points with values in \(\bar{k}\), which is quite clear. The last assertion follows from the fact that every lifting of \(\mathrm{H}''\) into H, being étale over \(k\), is reduced, hence is necessarily contained in \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}}\).
Note that \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{p^n}\) is a multiple extension of groups isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\). We then deduce from 1.5 the corollary:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.7

For an algebraic group G defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\) to be unipotent, it is necessary and sufficient that it possess a composition series whose successive quotients are isomorphic to one of the groups \(\mathbb{G}_a\), \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\), \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) if \(p > 0\). (We shall call these groups the elementary unipotent groups.)
2. First properties of unipotent groups

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

A unipotent algebraic group defined over a field \(k\) is affine over \(k\).

By (fpqc) descent of affine morphisms, it suffices to prove 2.1 when \(k\) is algebraically closed. In that case, G is, by definition, a multiple extension of affine algebraic groups, hence is affine, by Exp. VIB 9.2 (viii) applied to affine morphisms.2

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2

i) The property of an algebraic group being unipotent is invariant under extension of the base field.

ii) Every algebraic subgroup of a unipotent group is unipotent.

iii) Every algebraic quotient group of a unipotent group is unipotent.

iv) Every extension of a unipotent algebraic group by a unipotent algebraic group is one as well.
Proof. i) follows immediately from 1.3 and 1.2. To establish the other properties, we may assume the field \(k\) algebraically closed. Then iv) is evident from definition 1.3.

So let G be a unipotent algebraic group, \(\mathrm{G}'\) an algebraic subgroup of G, \(\mathrm{G}''\) an algebraic quotient group of G, and \(\mathrm{G}_i\) (\(i = 1, \ldots, n\)) a composition series of G such that \(\mathrm{H}_i = \mathrm{G}_i/\mathrm{G}_{i+1}\) is an elementary unipotent group (1.7).

To prove ii), consider the composition series of \(\mathrm{G}'\) induced by that of G: \(\mathrm{G}'_i = \mathrm{G}_i \cap \mathrm{G}'\). The group \(\mathrm{G}'_i/\mathrm{G}'_{i+1}\) identifies with an algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}_i\), hence is isomorphic to a subgroup of \(\mathbb{G}_a\), and consequently \(\mathrm{G}'\) is unipotent.

To prove iii), consider the composition series of \(\mathrm{G}''\) that is the image of that of G: \(\mathrm{G}''_i =\) image of \(\mathrm{G}_i\) in \(\mathrm{G}''\). The group \(\mathrm{G}''_i/\mathrm{G}''_{i+1}\) is then a quotient of \(\mathrm{H}_i\) and it suffices to prove the lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3

If H is an elementary unipotent group (1.7) defined over a field \(k\), every algebraic quotient group \(\mathrm{H}''\) of H is zero or is isomorphic to H over \(k\).

Proof. If \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_a\) in characteristic 0, or if \(\mathrm{H} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) or \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) (\(p > 0\)), it suffices to note that it follows from 1.5 that H has no algebraic subgroups other than 0 and H (observe that a nonzero algebraic subgroup of \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) is defined by a \(k\)-algebra of rank over \(k\) at least equal to \(p\) (1.5 iii), hence is equal to \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\)).

Now let \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_a\) (\(p > 0\)), so that we have an exact sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{N} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_a \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}'' \longrightarrow 0. \] If \(\mathrm{N} = \mathbb{G}_a\), then \(\mathrm{H}'' = 0\). Otherwise, proceeding by induction on the length of a composition series of N, we may assume that \(\mathrm{N} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\), or that N is a form of \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^r\) (1.5).

a) If \(\mathrm{N} \simeq \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\), the proof of 1.5 iii) shows that N is necessarily the kernel of the Frobenius morphism F in \(\mathbb{G}_a\) and we conclude by means of the exact sequence: \[ 0 \to \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p \to \mathbb{G}_a \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{F}\ } \mathbb{G}_a \to 0 \]

b) If \(\mathrm{N} \simeq \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\), it is immediate that there exists \(a \in k^*\) such that N is the closed subscheme of \(\mathbb{G}_a = \operatorname{Spec} k[\mathrm{X}]\) defined by the equation \(\mathrm{X}^p - a\mathrm{X} = 0\). We then conclude by means of the exact sequence: \[ 0 \to \mathrm{N} \to \mathbb{G}_a \xrightarrow{\ \mathfrak{P}\ } \mathbb{G}_a \to 0, \] where \(\mathfrak{P}\) is the Artin-Schreier morphism: \(x \mapsto x^p - ax\).

c) If N is a form of \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^r\), there exists a finite Galois extension \(k'\) of \(k\) that trivializes N. By b) and an obvious induction on \(r\), \(\mathbb{G}_a/\mathrm{N}\) is a form of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) trivialized by \(k'\). It then suffices to apply the following lemma:

blocks.label: 2.3 bis
blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3 bis

Let \(k\) be a field, G a \(k\)-algebraic group which is a form of \(\mathbb{G}_a\), trivialized by a finite extension \(k'\) separable over \(k\). Then G is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\).

Indeed, the group of \(k'\)-automorphisms of the algebraic group \((\mathbb{G}_a)_{k'}\) is the group of nonzero homotheties \((k')^\times\) (Bible, Exp. 9, Lemma 1) and the Galois cohomology group \(\mathrm{H}^1(k'/k, \mathbb{G}_m)\) is zero (we may assume that \(k'\) is a Galois extension of \(k\)), by Hilbert's Theorem 90. Lemma 2.3 bis then follows from the classification of \(k\)-forms of an algebraic group (cf. J.-P. Serre, Cohomologie galoisienne, Chap. III, 1.3).
This completes the proof of 2.2.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4

Let \(k\) be a field, M a \(k\)-group of multiplicative type (Exp. VIII) and of finite type, U a unipotent \(k\)-algebraic group. Then:

i) \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{k\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{U}) = \underline{e}\) (a fortiori \(\operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{U}) = e\)).

ii) \(\operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{M}) = e\).

To prove i) we must establish that for every prescheme S over \(k\), \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{M}_\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{U}_\mathrm{S}) = e\). But this follows from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.5

Let S be a prescheme, M an S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, U an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S with unipotent fibres; then \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{U}) = e\).

Indeed, under the hypotheses made, for an S-group morphism \(u : \mathrm{M} \to \mathrm{U}\) to be the unit homomorphism, it suffices that the restriction of \(u\) to the fibres of M over the points of S be the zero morphism (Exp. IX 5.2). We are thus reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field, which we may moreover assume algebraically closed. In view of definition 1.1, we may restrict to \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{G}_a\), in which case the property has already been proved (Exp. XII 4.4.1).

Let us now prove 2.4 ii). So let \(u : \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{M}\) be a \(k\)-group morphism. The image \(u(\mathrm{U})\) is representable by an algebraic subgroup \(\mathrm{U}''\) of M (Exp. VIB 5.4).3 The group \(\mathrm{U}''\) is unipotent as a quotient of a unipotent group (2.2 iii)) and is of multiplicative type as a subgroup of a group of multiplicative type (cf. Bible, Exp. 4, Th. 2 cor. 1, or Exp. IX 6.8), hence \(\mathrm{U}''\) is the unit group by 2.4 i).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.6

Keeping the notation of 2.4, it is no longer true in general that the functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{k\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{M})\) is equal to \(\underline{e}\). Indeed, take a prescheme S such that \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S})\) contains a nonzero element \(\varepsilon\) with \(\varepsilon^2 = 0\) (for example the spectrum of the algebra of dual numbers over a ring A). For every \(\mathrm{S}'\) over S, the map \(u \mapsto 1 + \varepsilon_{\mathrm{S}'} u\) defines a homomorphism, functorial in \(\mathrm{S}'\), from the additive group \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'})\) to the multiplicative group \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}^\times)\), hence defines an S-group morphism \((\mathbb{G}_a)_\mathrm{S} \to (\mathbb{G}_m)_\mathrm{S}\), and since \(\varepsilon \neq 0\), this morphism is not zero.

Recall (Exp. VIIA §3) that when G is a commutative S-group prescheme, finite and flat over S, we have Cartier duality at our disposal, and G is reflexive in the sense of Exp. VIII §1. More precisely, the functor \(\mathrm{G} \mapsto \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathbb{G}_m)\) is representable by a commutative S-group prescheme \(\mathrm{D}(\mathrm{G})\), finite and flat over S, and the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{D}(\mathrm{D}(\mathrm{G}))\) is an isomorphism. In particular, we obtain:
(i) \(\mathrm{D}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \simeq \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) and consequently \(\mathrm{D}(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}) \simeq \boldsymbol{\mu}_n\).
(ii) If S is of characteristic \(p > 0\), \(\mathrm{D}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p) \simeq \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\).

pages.title: 3. Unipotent groups acting on a vector space

blocks.html: 3. Unipotent groups acting on a vector space

Recall (Exp. I 4.6.1) that if S is a prescheme and M a sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-modules, we denote by \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{M})\) the S-functor \((\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^\circ \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) defined by the condition \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{M})(\mathrm{S}') = \Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathrm{M} \otimes \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'})\) for every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\).

Furthermore, recall that if an S-group acts on an S-functor \(\mathbf{V}\), one defines the S-functor \(\mathbf{V}^\mathrm{G}\) of invariants of \(\mathbf{V}\) under G as the subfunctor of \(\mathbf{V}\) whose set of points with values in an \(\mathrm{S}'\) over S is the set of \(x \in \mathbf{V}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \(x_{\mathrm{S}''}\) is fixed under \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}'')\) for every \(\mathrm{S}''\) over \(\mathrm{S}'\).
This being said, we have the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, affine over S, defined by the quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-algebra A. Suppose that G acts on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-modules M, and let \(\mu : \mathrm{M} \to \mathrm{A} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{M}\) be the comorphism defining the action of G on M (Exp. I 4.7.2) and \(\nu : \mathrm{M} \to \mathrm{A} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{M}\) the morphism \(x \mapsto \mu(x) - 1 \otimes x\). Then:

i) \(\underline{\mathrm{M}}^\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}) = \Gamma \operatorname{Ker}(\nu)\).

ii) If S is the spectrum of a field \(k\), \(\underline{\mathrm{M}}^\mathrm{G}\) is of the form \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{N})\), where N is a vector subspace of M.

iii) If S is the spectrum of a field \(k\), every element \(x\) of M is contained in a vector subspace of M, of finite dimension over \(k\), stable under the action of G.
Proof. iii) is included for the record and has already been proved in Exp. VIB 11.2.

i) It is clear that \(\underline{\mathrm{M}}^\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) contains \(\Gamma \operatorname{Ker}( u)\). To establish the converse, we may assume S affine with ring B. Let \(m \in \underline{\mathrm{M}}^\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\). Then for every B-algebra \(\mathrm{B}'\) and every element \(u\) of \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{B} ext{-alg}}(\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}')\) (\(u\) corresponds to an element of \(\mathrm{G}(\operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{B}')\)), we have: \[ (u \otimes 1_\mathrm{M}) u(m) = 0 ext{ in } \mathrm{B}' \otimes_\mathrm{B} \mathrm{M}. \] Taking in particular \(\mathrm{B}' = \mathrm{A}\) and \(u\) the identity of A, we indeed find \( u(m) = 0\).

ii) Let N be the kernel of \(\nu\), equal to \(\underline{\mathrm{M}}^\mathrm{G}(k)\) by i). Every \(k\)-prescheme S is flat over \(k\), so we have: \(\underline{\mathrm{M}}^\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}) = \Gamma \operatorname{Ker}(\nu \otimes_k \mathrm{S}) = \Gamma(\mathrm{N} \otimes_k \mathrm{S})\). Hence \(\underline{\mathrm{M}}^\mathrm{G}\) is isomorphic to the functor \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{N})\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2

Let G be a unipotent algebraic group, defined over a field \(k\), acting on a \(k\)-vector space V. Then if \(\mathrm{V} \neq 0\), we have \(\mathrm{V}^\mathrm{G} \neq 0\).

In view of 3.1 ii) and iii), we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed and V of finite dimension over \(k\). Let \(0 \to \mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'' \to 0\) be an exact sequence of algebraic groups and suppose that G acts on a sheaf \(\mathbf{V}\) (for the fpqc topology). We have of course \(\mathbf{V}^\mathrm{G} \subset \mathbf{V}^{\mathrm{G}'}\), and the quotient presheaf \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}'\) acts in a natural way on \(\mathbf{V}^{\mathrm{G}'}\). But \(\mathbf{V}^{\mathrm{G}'}\) is a sheaf (as the kernel of the well-known pair of morphisms \(\mathbf{V} \rightrightarrows \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(\mathrm{G}', \mathbf{V})\)); consequently, the sheaf associated with \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}'\), that is \(\mathrm{G}''\), acts on \(\mathbf{V}^{\mathrm{G}'}\), and it is immediate to check that \((\mathbf{V}^{\mathrm{G}'})^{\mathrm{G}''} = \mathbf{V}^\mathrm{G} = (\mathbf{V}^{\mathrm{G}'})^{\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}'}\). This remark allows us to reduce, in order to prove 3.2, to the case where G is an elementary unipotent group (1.7).

a) \(\mathrm{G} = \mathbb{G}_a\), \(p = 0\). It follows from (BIBLE 4 prop. 4) that a morphism from \(\mathbb{G}_a\) to the linear group \(\mathrm{GL}(\mathrm{V})\) is given by an exponential map: \[ \mathrm{T} \mapsto \sum_{q=0}^{\infty} \mathrm{T}^q \frac{n^q}{q!} \] where \(n\) is a nilpotent endomorphism of V. But then \(\mathrm{V} \neq 0 \Rightarrow \operatorname{Ker} n \neq 0\), and it is clear that every vector of V killed by \(n\) is left fixed by G.
Suppose now \(p > 0\).

b) \(\mathrm{G} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\). The group \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) being a radicial group of height 1 (Exp. VIIA §7), giving a representation of \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) in V amounts to giving a representation of the \(p\)-Lie algebra \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) in \(\mathfrak{gl}(\mathrm{V})\) (App. II 2.2), that is, here, to giving an element X of \(\operatorname{End}(\mathrm{V})\) such that \(\mathrm{X}^p = 0\) (App. II 2.1). But then \(\mathrm{V} \neq 0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{W} = \operatorname{Ker}(\mathrm{X}) \neq 0\), and again by (App. II 2.2), we have \(\mathrm{W} = \mathrm{V}^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p}\).

c) \(\mathrm{G} = \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\). A representation of G in V is equivalent to the datum of an element \(x\) of \(\operatorname{Aut}(\mathrm{V})\) such that \(x^p = 1\), i.e. \((1 - x)^p = 0\), so \(x\) is of the form \(1 + n\), with \(n\) nilpotent, and \(\mathrm{W} = \operatorname{Ker} n\) is left fixed by \(x\).

d) \(\mathrm{G} = \mathbb{G}_a\). Let \(\mathrm{G}_i\), \(i \in \mathrm{I}\), be the increasing filtered family of étale algebraic subgroups of \(\mathbb{G}_a\), hence isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^{r_i}\) (prop. 1.5), and let \(\mathrm{V}_i = \mathrm{V}^{\mathrm{G}_i}\). Since V is of finite nonzero dimension, and \(\mathrm{V}_i\) is nonzero by c), the decreasing filtered family of the \(\mathrm{V}_i\) is stationary, and \(\mathrm{W} = \bigcap_{i \in \mathrm{I}} \mathrm{V}_i \neq 0\). Now we have the lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

The family of étale subgroups of \((\mathbb{G}_a)_\mathrm{S}\) (S a prescheme of characteristic \(p > 0\)) is schematically dense in G (Exp. IX 4.1).

By Exp. IX 4.4, it suffices to prove the lemma when S is the spectrum of the prime field \(\mathbb{F}_p\). In this case, it suffices to consider the family of étale subgroups \(\mathrm{G}_n\) (\(n \geqslant 1\)) with equation \(\mathrm{X}^{p^n} - \mathrm{X} = 0\), which is schematically dense in \((\mathbb{G}_a)_{\mathbb{F}_p}\) since it contains every closed point.

This being so, let us return to the proof of 3.2 d). If \(w \in \mathrm{W}\), its stabilizer in G is an algebraic subgroup of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) which dominates \(\mathrm{G}_i\) for every \(i \in \mathrm{I}\), hence is equal to \(\mathbb{G}_a\) (3.3), and consequently \(\mathrm{W} = \mathrm{V}^\mathrm{G}\).
From 3.2 we immediately deduce the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Let \(k\) be a field, G a unipotent \(k\)-algebraic group acting on a finite-dimensional \(k\)-vector space V. Then V possesses a chain of vector subspaces \(\mathrm{V}_i\), defined over \(k\), stable under G, \[ 0 = \mathrm{V}_0 \subset \mathrm{V}_1 \subset \ldots \subset \mathrm{V}_n = \mathrm{V}, \] such that G acts trivially on \(\mathrm{V}_{i+1}/\mathrm{V}_i\). One may moreover assume \(\mathrm{V}_{i+1}/\mathrm{V}_i\) of dimension 1.
We shall now summarize and complete the properties of unipotent groups already proved, in the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.5

Let G be an algebraic group defined over a field \(k\). The following properties are equivalent:

i) G is unipotent.

ii) G possesses a composition series, defined over \(k\), whose successive quotients are isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\) if \(p = 0\) (resp. to \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\), \(\mathbb{G}_a\), or twisted \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^r\) (1.4) if \(p > 0\)).

iii) As in ii), but the composition series is moreover assumed central.

iv) G possesses a characteristic composition series (Exp. VIB) defined over \(k\), whose successive quotients are isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\) if \(p = 0\) (resp. to \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r\), twisted \((\mathbb{G}_a)^s\) or twisted \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^t\), taken in that order, if \(p > 0\)).

v) G is isomorphic to an algebraic subgroup of the group \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)_k\) of strictly upper triangular matrices of the linear group \(\mathrm{GL}(n)_k\), for a suitable integer \(n \geqslant 0\).

vi) G is affine and for every linear representation of G in a nonzero finite-dimensional \(k\)-vector space V, we have \(\mathrm{V}^\mathrm{G} \neq 0\).
Proof.
i) \(\Rightarrow\) vi) by 2.1 and 3.2.

vi) \(\Rightarrow\) v). The algebraic group G being affine, G is an algebraic subgroup of a suitable linear group \(\mathrm{GL}(\mathrm{V})\) (Exp. VIB 11.3). Applying 3.4 to the representation of G in V defined by this embedding, we obtain v).

v) \(\Rightarrow\) iii). We know that the algebraic group \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\) possesses a central composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\). The composition series induced on G gives property iii), in view of 1.5.
iii) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i) and iv) \(\Rightarrow\) i) are clear.
We shall prove i) \(\Rightarrow\) iv) in a moment, but let us already note some consequences of what has been proved.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.6

We shall say that a \(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) (\(p > 0\)) (cf. Exp. VIIA §5) is unipotent if the map \(x \mapsto x^{(p)}\) is nilpotent, i.e. if for every \(x \in \mathfrak{g}\) there exists an integer \(n > 0\) such that \(x^{(p^n)} = 0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

A unipotent algebraic group G is nilpotent (Exp. VIB §8); its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent (Bourbaki, Groupes et algèbres de Lie, Chap. 1 §4) and is isomorphic to a Lie algebra of nilpotent endomorphisms of a finite-dimensional vector space. In characteristic \(p > 0\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) is a unipotent \(p\)-Lie algebra (3.6).

Since i) \(\Rightarrow\) v), it suffices to prove 3.7 when \(\mathrm{G} = \operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\). We have already used the fact that \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\) is a nilpotent algebraic group. Moreover the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\) of \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\) consists of the upper triangular endomorphisms of V with zeros on the main diagonal. They are therefore nilpotent and consequently \(\mathfrak{h}\) is nilpotent (Bourbaki, loc. cit. Chap. 1 §4. cor. 3). If \(p > 0\), since the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power in the \(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{gl}(\mathrm{V}) = \operatorname{End}(\mathrm{V})\) coincides with the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power of endomorphisms of V (Exp. VIIA 6.4.4), we see that \(\mathfrak{h}\) is unipotent.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and let G be a smooth affine algebraic group over \(k\). Then the following properties are equivalent:

i) G is unipotent.

ii) \(\mathrm{G}(k)\) consists of unipotent elements (BIBLE 4 prop. 4 cor. 1), that is, G is unipotent in the sense of BIBLE.
i) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) since G is isomorphic to an algebraic subgroup of a group \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\) by 3.5 i) \(\Rightarrow\) v).

ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i). So let G be a unipotent algebraic group in the sense of BIBLE and denote by \(\mathrm{G}^0\) its identity component. The maximal tori of \(\mathrm{G}^0\), consisting of unipotent elements, are trivial, so \(\mathrm{G}^0\) is equal to its Cartan subgroups. Consequently, \(\mathrm{G}^0\) is solvable (BIBLE 6 Th. 6), hence is triangularizable (BIBLE 6. Th. 1). In short, \(\mathrm{G}^0\) is an algebraic subgroup of a group \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\), so it is unipotent in the sense of this exposé.

The group \((\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}^0)(k)\) is a finite group consisting of unipotent elements; it is therefore zero if \(p = 0\) and equal to a finite \(p\)-group if \(p > 0\) (BIBLE 4 prop. 4). But then \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}^0\) is unipotent in the sense of this exposé, being a multiple extension of groups isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\). This proves that G is unipotent.
End of the proof of 3.5. Let us prove that i) \(\Rightarrow\) iv).

a) \(p > 0\). Consider the increasing chain of algebraic subgroups of G: \[ \{e\} \subset {}_\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{G}) \subset {}_{\mathrm{F}^2}(\mathrm{G}) \subset \cdots \subset {}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G}) \subset \mathrm{G}^0 \subset \mathrm{G}. \] We thus obtain a characteristic composition series of G (App. II 1) and for \(n\) large enough, \(\mathrm{G}/{}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G})\) is smooth (App. II 3.1), so that the successive quotients are, in order: (1) radicial groups of height 1, (2) a smooth connected group, (3) an étale group.
To prove i) \(\Rightarrow\) iv) it therefore suffices to prove the:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.9

Let G be a unipotent algebraic group defined over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p > 0\). Then G possesses a characteristic composition series, defined over \(k\), whose successive quotients are isomorphic to:

i) \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r\) if G is radicial.

ii) twisted \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\) if G is smooth and connected.

iii) twisted \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^r\) if G is étale.

Proof. i) The group G is radicial. Filtering G by the \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G})\), we reduce to the case where G is radicial of height 1. Since G is nilpotent (3.5 i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii)) and the centre of an algebraic group is representable (Exp. VIII 6.5 e)), we may consider the ascending central series of G, obviously characteristic in G, which reduces us to the case where G is moreover commutative. Let \(\mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie}(\mathrm{G})\). The \(p^{\text{th}}\) power morphism \(\pi\) is then additive in \(\mathfrak{g}\) (Exp. VIIA); we shall reduce to the case where it is zero. For every prescheme S over \(k\), set \(\mathfrak{g}_\mathrm{S} = \mathfrak{g} \otimes_k \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) and let \(\mathfrak{h}_\mathrm{S}\) be the subsheaf of abelian groups of \(\mathfrak{g}_\mathrm{S}\) which is the image of \(\mathfrak{g}_\mathrm{S}\) under \(\pi_\mathrm{S}\). Finally let \(\overline{\mathfrak{h}}_\mathrm{S}\) be the subsheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-modules of \(\mathfrak{g}_\mathrm{S}\) generated by \(\mathfrak{h}_\mathrm{S}\). It is clear that \(\overline{\mathfrak{h}}_\mathrm{S} = \overline{\mathfrak{h}}_k \otimes_k \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) and that \(\overline{\mathfrak{h}}_\mathrm{S}\)4 is a characteristic sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra of \(\mathfrak{g}_\mathrm{S}\) (that is, it is stable under the S-functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{p\text{-Lie}}(\mathfrak{g})\)). It then follows from App. II 2.2 that \(\overline{\mathfrak{h}}_k\) is the Lie algebra of an algebraic subgroup H of G, characteristic in G. Moreover, taking into account 3.7 and lemma 3.9 bis below, if \(\mathrm{G} \neq \{e\}\), H is distinct from G, since \(\overline{\mathfrak{h}}_k\) is distinct from \(\mathfrak{g}\). Furthermore, if \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{G}''\), we have \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}'' = \mathfrak{g}/\overline{\mathfrak{h}}_k\) (App. II 2.2), and consequently the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power is zero in \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}''\). Proceeding by induction on \(\dim \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\), we are thus reduced to the case where \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\) is a \(p\)-Lie algebra in which the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power is zero. But then \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}''\) is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Lie}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r\) for a suitable integer \(r \geqslant 0\) (App. II 2.1) and consequently (App. II 2.2), \(\mathrm{G}''\) is isomorphic to \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r\). It remains to prove the

blocks.label: 3.9 bis
blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.9 bis

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p > 0\), \(\mathfrak{g}\) a commutative, unipotent (3.6) \(p\)-Lie algebra of finite dimension over \(k\), and \(\mathfrak{h}\) the sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) generated by the image of the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power map in \(\mathfrak{g}\). Then if \(\mathfrak{g} \neq 0\), we have \(\mathfrak{h} \neq \mathfrak{g}\).

Indeed, since \(\mathfrak{g}\) is commutative, \(\mathfrak{h}\) is simply the \(k\)-vector subspace of \(\mathfrak{g}\) generated by the \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)}\) (\(\mathrm{X} \in \mathfrak{g}\)). If \(\mathfrak{g}\) is \(\neq 0\) and is unipotent, there exists \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathfrak{g}\), \(\mathrm{X} \neq 0\), such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = 0\). Let \(\mathrm{X}_1, \ldots, \mathrm{X}_n\) be a basis of a complement in \(\mathfrak{g}\) of the line \(k\mathrm{X}\). The Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\) is then the \(k\)-vector subspace of \(\mathfrak{g}\) generated by \(\mathrm{X}_1^{(p)}, \ldots, \mathrm{X}_n^{(p)}\), hence is of dimension at most \(n = \dim \mathfrak{g} - 1\).

Proof of 3.9 ii) G is smooth and connected. In this case, the descending central series of G is representable by smooth connected characteristic algebraic subgroups \(\mathrm{G}_i\) (Exp. VIB 8.3 and 7.4), and \(\mathrm{G}_i = 0\) for \(i\) large enough, since G is nilpotent (3.5 i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii)). It suffices to prove 3.9 for the groups \(\mathrm{G}_i/\mathrm{G}_{i+1}\), which reduces us to the case where G is moreover commutative. For every integer \(n > 0\), let \(\mathrm{G}^n\) be the algebraic subgroup of G which is the image of G under the morphism of raising to the \(p^n\)-th power. The group \(\mathrm{G}^n\) is thus smooth, connected and characteristic, and it follows from the definition 1.1 of unipotent groups that \(\mathrm{G}^n = 0\) for \(n\) large enough. Replacing G by \(\mathrm{G}^n/\mathrm{G}^{n+1}\), we may moreover assume that G is killed by raising to the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power. But then, by (J.-P. Serre, Groupes algébriques et corps de classes, chap. VII, prop. 11), G is a form of \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\), for a suitable integer \(r\).

Proof of 3.9 iii) G is étale. Proceeding as in ii), we reduce to the case where G is commutative, then to the case where G is killed by \(p\); but then \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^r\).

Proof of 3.5 i) \(\Rightarrow\) iv) in case b) \(p = 0\). The group G is then smooth and connected, and proceeding as in 3.9 ii), we reduce to the case where G is moreover commutative. We then have the following more precise result:

blocks.label: 3.9 ter
blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.9 ter

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic 0, G a commutative unipotent \(k\)-algebraic group, \(\mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\). Then there exists a canonical isomorphism: \[ \exp : \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{G} \] The morphism \(\exp\) is the unique homomorphism \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}) \to \mathrm{G}\) inducing the identity on the Lie algebras.

Since G is unipotent, G can be realized as an algebraic subgroup of \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\) for a suitable integer \(n\) (3.5 i) \(\Rightarrow\) v)). The choice of such an embedding allows us to identify \(\mathfrak{g}\) with a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{gl}(n)\) consisting of nilpotent endomorphisms. Whence a \(k\)-morphism: \[ \exp : \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{GL}(n), \qquad \mathrm{T} \mapsto \sum_{i \geqslant 0} \frac{\mathrm{T}^i}{i!}. \] Since G is commutative, the morphism \(\exp\) is a homomorphism. Let \(\mathrm{G}'\) be the algebraic group image of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) under the morphism \(\exp\). If we canonically identify \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) with \(\mathfrak{g}\), the tangent linear map to \(\exp\) is simply the injection \(\mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{gl}(n)\). Consequently \(\operatorname{Lie}(\mathrm{G} \cap \mathrm{G}') = \mathfrak{g} \cap \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{g}\). Since \(\mathrm{G} \cap \mathrm{G}'\) is smooth (Exp. VIB 1.6.1) and G connected (being a multiple extension of groups \(\mathbb{G}_a\) (3.5 i) \(\Rightarrow\) ii))), we necessarily have \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{G}'\). The kernel \(\operatorname{Ker}(\exp)\) is an étale unipotent group, hence is the unit group, and consequently \(\exp\) is an isomorphism from \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) onto G.

If \(h : \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}) \to \mathrm{G}\) is another homomorphism such that \(\operatorname{Lie}(h)\) is the identity map of \(\mathfrak{g}\), the morphism \(h - \exp\) is a homomorphism (G is commutative), so its tangent linear map is zero. Since \(k\) is of characteristic 0 and \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) connected, it again follows from Cartier's theorem that we necessarily have \(h = \exp\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.10

Let G be an algebraic group defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Then the following properties are equivalent:

i) Every morphism from a group of multiplicative type M to G is the zero morphism.

i bis) G has no nonzero subgroups of multiplicative type.

ii) a) if \(p = 0\): \(\mathrm{G}(k)\) contains no points of finite order other than \(e\);
b) if \(p \neq 0\): for every prime number \(q \neq p\), \(x \in \mathrm{G}(k)\) and \(x^q = e\) implies \(x = e\); for every \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\) such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}\), we have \(\mathrm{X} = 0\).

ii bis) a) if \(p = 0\): as in ii) a);
b) if \(p \neq 0\): every finite subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}(k)\) is a \(p\)-group; for every \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\) such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}\), we have \(\mathrm{X} = 0\).
Proof. i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) i bis), since the image of a group of multiplicative type is of multiplicative type (Exp. IX 2.7).

ii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) ii bis). For if an ordinary finite group H has order which is not a power of \(p\), there exist a prime number \(q \neq p\) and an element \(x\) of H distinct from \(e\) such that \(x^q = e\) (Sylow's theorem, cf. J.-P. Serre, Corps locaux, chap. IX §2).
i) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) follows from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.11

Let G be an algebraic group defined over a field \(k\).

a) If \(k\) contains the \(n^{\text{th}}\) roots of unity, \(n\) an integer prime to \(p\), we have: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n, \mathrm{G}) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}, \mathrm{G}) \simeq {}_n\mathrm{G}(k) \quad \text{(points of order } n \text{ of } \mathrm{G}(k)). \]

b) If \(p > 0\), \(\operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p, \mathrm{G}) \simeq \{\mathrm{X} \in \mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}, \text{ such that } \mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}\}\).
Indeed, to prove a) we note that \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) is then isomorphic over \(k\) to \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})\), and b) is a consequence of App. II 2.1.
ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i bis). By lemma 3.11, ii) is equivalent to the fact that for every prime number \(r\), G contains no subgroups \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_r\), which implies i bis) on account of the:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.12

Let G be a diagonalizable S-group, of finite type over S and distinct from the unit group. Then there exist a prime number \(r\) and a subgroup of G isomorphic to \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_r)_\mathrm{S}\).

Let \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{D}_\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{M})\), where M is an abelian group of finite type, hence an extension of a free group \(\mathrm{M}''\) by a finite group \(\mathrm{M}'\). If \(\mathrm{M}'' \neq 0\), it is clear that M admits quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/r\mathbb{Z}\) for every integer \(r\). If \(\mathrm{M}'' = 0\), \(\mathrm{M}'\) admits a quotient isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/r\mathbb{Z}\) for every prime number \(r\) dividing the order of M. The lemma follows by duality.
We have seen (prop. 2.4) that a unipotent group satisfies the equivalent conditions of 3.10. The aim of the next section is to prove the converse.
4. A characterization of unipotent groups

As announced, we shall show that an algebraic group G defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\), containing no nonzero subgroup of multiplicative type, is unipotent. In fact, it suffices that it contain no quite particular "elementary" subgroups of multiplicative type, which depend on the hypotheses made on G. Before stating the general theorem, let us study some special cases in detail.
4.1. Smooth, connected, affine algebraic groups.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1.1

Let \(k\) be a field, G a smooth, connected, affine \(k\)-algebraic group, \(\mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\). Then the following properties are equivalent:

i) G is unipotent.

ii) G possesses a central composition series whose successive quotients are forms of \(\mathbb{G}_a\).

iii) G possesses a central, characteristic composition series whose successive quotients are forms of \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\).

iv) There exists an integer \(n > 1\) such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) contains no subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\).

v) Every maximal torus of G is the unit group.

Suppose moreover that G is an algebraic subgroup of a linear group \(\mathrm{GL}(n)\). Then the preceding conditions are also equivalent to:

vi) \(\mathfrak{g} \subset \mathfrak{gl}(n)\) consists of nilpotent endomorphisms.

vii) \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent and its centre contains no nonzero semisimple endomorphism.
Proof. ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i) is clear and i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii) was seen in 3.9. The implication iii) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) will follow from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1.2

Let \(k\) be a field, G a \(k\)-algebraic group which is a form of \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\). Then:

a) G can be realized as an algebraic subgroup of the group \((\mathbb{G}_a)^n\) for a suitable integer \(n\).

b) G possesses a composition series whose successive quotients are forms of \(\mathbb{G}_a\).

Indeed, by hypothesis, there exists an extension \(k'\) of \(k\) such that \(\mathrm{G}_{k'}\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_{a, k'})^r\). By the principle of finite extension (EGA IV 9.1.1), we may assume that \(k'\) is a finite extension of \(k\). But then, for a), it suffices to consider the canonical closed immersion (EGA V5): \[ \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \prod_{k'/k} (\mathrm{G}_{k'})/k' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\mathbb{G}_{a, k})^n \quad (\text{with } n = r \deg(k'/k)). \] To prove b), taking a) into account, we may assume that G is a closed subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}' = (\mathbb{G}_a)^n\). If \(\mathrm{G} \neq 0\), there exists a hyperplane \(\mathfrak{h}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}' = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}'\) not containing \(\mathfrak{g}\). Let H be the vector subgroup \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\) of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}') = \mathrm{G}'\). Since H is defined by one equation in \(\mathrm{G}'\), \(\mathrm{H} \cap \mathrm{G}\) is defined by one equation in G and we have the inequalities: \[ \dim \mathrm{G} - 1 \leqslant \dim(\mathrm{G} \cap \mathrm{H}) \leqslant \dim \operatorname{Lie}(\mathrm{G} \cap \mathrm{H}) = \dim_k(\mathfrak{g} \cap \mathfrak{h}) = \dim_k(\mathfrak{g}) - 1 = \dim \mathrm{G} - 1 \] Whence \(\dim(\mathrm{G} \cap \mathrm{H}) = \dim \operatorname{Lie}(\mathrm{G} \cap \mathrm{H})\) and consequently \(\mathrm{G} \cap \mathrm{H}\) is smooth. The group \(\mathrm{G}_1 = (\mathrm{G} \cap \mathrm{H})^0\) is an algebraic subgroup of G, smooth and connected, such that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}_1\) is smooth, connected, of dimension 1, hence is a form of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) (4.1 i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii)). We finish by induction on the dimension of G.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.1.3

If \(k\) is a perfect field, a smooth connected \(k\)-algebraic group is unipotent if and only if it possesses a composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\).
Continuation of the proof of 4.1.
i) \(\Rightarrow\) iv) by 2.4 i).

iv) \(\Rightarrow\) v). By Exp. XIV 4.1, G possesses a maximal torus T defined over \(k\). Now if \(r = \dim \mathrm{T}\), \(({}_n\mathrm{T})_{\bar{k}}\) is isomorphic to \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)^r\). Hence \(r = 0\).
v) \(\Rightarrow\) i) as remarked in the proof of 3.8.

i) \(\Rightarrow\) vi). By 3.4, G is in fact contained in an algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{GL}(n)\) isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\), hence \(\mathfrak{g}\) consists of nilpotent endomorphisms.
vi) \(\Rightarrow\) vii). Indeed, \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent by Engel's theorem (Bourbaki, Groupes et algèbres de Lie, chap. I §4 cor. 3).

vii) \(\Rightarrow\) v). Let T be a maximal subtorus of G (Exp. XIV 1.1), \(\mathfrak{t}\) its Lie algebra. The embedding of G in \(\mathrm{GL}(n)\) defines a representation of T which is necessarily semisimple (this can be seen over an algebraic closure \(\bar{k}\) of \(k\), applying Exp. I 4.7.3). So if \(\mathrm{X} \in \mathfrak{t}\), X is a semisimple endomorphism in \(\mathfrak{gl}(n)\). One sees immediately that this implies that the map: \[ \operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X} : \mathrm{Y} \longmapsto [\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}] \] is a semisimple endomorphism of \(\mathfrak{gl}(n)\), hence of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Since on the other hand this endomorphism is nilpotent, \(\mathfrak{g}\) being nilpotent, \(\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}\) is zero, so X is central. But then \(\mathfrak{t}\) is central and consists of semisimple endomorphisms, hence is zero by hypothesis; a fortiori, T is the unit group.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.1.4

a) We shall give later (4.3.1) an infinitesimal characterization of unipotent groups in characteristic \(p > 0\), which is independent of an embedding in \(\mathrm{GL}(n)\).

b) When \(k\) is perfect, conditions ii) and iii) of 4.1.1 simplify, by reason of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1.5

If \(k\) is a perfect field, every \(k\)-algebraic group G which is a form of \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\).

The lemma follows from 3.9 ter if the characteristic \(p\) of \(k\) is zero, and from 2.3 bis if \(r = 1\). In the general case (\(p > 0\)), let us realize G as an algebraic subgroup of \((\mathbb{G}_a)^n\) for a suitable integer \(n\) (4.1.2) and argue by induction on the integer \(n - r\). If \(r = n\) we indeed have \(\mathrm{G} = (\mathbb{G}_a)^r\). Otherwise the quotient group \(\mathbb{G}_a^n/\mathrm{G}\) is a smooth connected nonzero unipotent group which, in view of 4.1.1 i) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) and 2.3 bis, possesses a composition series with quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\). We deduce that there exists an algebraic subgroup \(\mathrm{G}_1\) of \((\mathbb{G}_a)^n\), smooth and connected, containing G, such that \(\mathbb{G}_a^n/\mathrm{G}_1 = \mathbb{G}_a\). By induction it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{G}_1\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_a)^{n-1}\). Now it is immediate to check that a homomorphism from \(\operatorname{Spec} k[\mathrm{X}_1, \ldots, \mathrm{X}_n] = \mathbb{G}_a^n\) to \(\mathbb{G}_a = \operatorname{Spec} k[\mathrm{T}]\) is defined by an additive polynomial of the form: \[ \sum_{i,j} a_{i,j} \mathrm{X}_i^{p^j}. \] Since \(\mathrm{G}_1\) is smooth, the linear part of this polynomial is not zero. After possibly making a linear change of the coordinates \(\mathrm{X}_i\), we may assume that \(\mathrm{G}_1\) is an algebraic subgroup of \((\mathbb{G}_a)^n\) defined by the equation: \[ (\ast) \quad \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}) = -\mathrm{X}_1 + \sum_{j=1}^{q} a_j \mathrm{X}_1^{p^j} + \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{X}_2, \ldots, \mathrm{X}_n) = 0, \qquad \text{with} \quad \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{X}_2, \ldots, \mathrm{X}_n) = \sum_{\substack{i > 1 \\ j > 0}} b_{i,j} \mathrm{X}_i^{p^j}. \] Let us then proceed by induction on the degree of P. If \(\deg \mathrm{P} = 1\), it is clear that \(\mathrm{G}_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbb{G}_a^{n-1}\). Otherwise, since \(k\) is perfect, we have \(\mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{X}) = \mathrm{Q}_1(\mathrm{X})^p\) and we can define an endomorphism \(v\) of \(\mathbb{G}_a^n\) by the formulas: \[ \mathrm{X}_i \mapsto \mathrm{X}_i \text{ for } i > 1, \qquad \mathrm{X}_1 \mapsto \sum_{j=1}^{q} a_j^{1/p} \mathrm{X}_1^{p^{j-1}} + \mathrm{Q}_1(\mathrm{X}). \] It is clear that \(v\) induces an isomorphism on \(\mathrm{G}_1\) and that \(v(\mathrm{G}_1)\) has for equation in \(\mathbb{G}_a^n\): \[ (\ast)_1 \quad \mathrm{P}_1(\mathrm{X}) = -\mathrm{X}_1 + \sum_{j=1}^{q} a_j^{1/p} \mathrm{X}_1^{p^j} + \mathrm{Q}_1(\mathrm{X}_2, \ldots, \mathrm{X}_n) = 0. \] Let us then distinguish two cases:
1st case. \(\deg(\mathrm{Q}) = \deg \mathrm{P} > p^q\). Then we have \(\deg \mathrm{P}_1 < \deg \mathrm{P}\) and we win by the induction hypothesis.

2nd case. \(\deg \mathrm{P} = p^q\) (\(a_q \neq 0\)). We then have \(\deg \mathrm{P}_1 = \deg \mathrm{P} = p^q\) and \(\deg \mathrm{Q}_1 < p^q\). (We cannot have \(\mathrm{Q} = 0\), otherwise \(\mathrm{G}_1\) would not be connected.) If the polynomial \(\mathrm{Q}_1\) has no linear part, we can repeat the preceding transformation. Continuing the process, we finally obtain an equation of the form: \[ (\ast)_s \quad \mathrm{P}_s(\mathrm{X}) = -\mathrm{X}_1 + \sum_{j=1}^{q} a_j^{1/p^s} \mathrm{X}_1^{p^j} + \mathrm{Q}_s(\mathrm{X}), \] where \(\mathrm{Q}_s(\mathrm{X}) = \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{X}_2, \ldots, \mathrm{X}_n)^{1/p^s}\) is an additive polynomial having a nonzero linear part, and moreover \(\deg \mathrm{Q}_s < p^q\). Suppose for example that the coefficient of \(\mathrm{X}_2\) in \(\mathrm{Q}_s\) is not zero, and let \(-\mathrm{L}\) be the linear part of \(\mathrm{P}_s(\mathrm{X})\). After possibly making a linear change of coordinates, the equation of \(\mathrm{G}_1\) becomes: \[ \mathrm{P}'(\mathrm{X}) = -\mathrm{L} + \sum_{j=1}^{q} a_j^{1/p^s} \mathrm{X}_1^{p^j} + \mathrm{Q}'(\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{X}_3, \ldots, \mathrm{X}_n), \] where \(\mathrm{Q}'\) is an additive polynomial, without linear part, and \(\deg(\mathrm{Q}') < p^q\). But then we are reduced to the first case6.
4.2. Radicial groups.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2.1

Let G be a radicial algebraic group defined over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p > 0\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

i) G is unipotent.

ii) G possesses a central composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\).

iii) G possesses a central and characteristic composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r\).

iv) \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) contains no subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\).

v) \(\mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\) is a unipotent \(p\)-Lie algebra (3.6).
Proof. iii) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i) is clear, i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii) is 3.9 i), and i) \(\Rightarrow\) iv) by 2.4 i).
We shall need the following lemma on abelian \(p\)-Lie algebras:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2.2

Let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be an abelian \(p\)-Lie algebra of finite dimension over a perfect field \(k\). Then \(\mathfrak{g}\) can be written in a unique way as the direct sum of a sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{r}\) on which the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power map is bijective and a unipotent (3.6) sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{u}\). (The algebra \(\mathfrak{r}\) will be called the reductive part of \(\mathfrak{g}\) and \(\mathfrak{u}\) the unipotent part.) The formation of \(\mathfrak{r}\) and \(\mathfrak{u}\) is compatible with extension of the field \(k\). If moreover \(k\) is algebraically closed, \(\mathfrak{r}\) admits a basis \(e_i\) such that \(e_i^{(p)} = e_i\).

The proof of this lemma is easy and left to the reader (cf. Bourbaki, Groupes et algèbres de Lie, chap. I §1 exercise 23). Let us simply say that \(\mathfrak{u}\) is the kernel of a suitable iterate of the map \(\mathrm{X} \mapsto \mathrm{X}^{(p)}\) and that \(\mathfrak{r}\) is the image of the same iterate.
This being so, let us prove iv) \(\Rightarrow\) v). After possibly extending the base field, we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed.

So let X be an element of \(\mathfrak{g}\) and \(\mathfrak{h}\) the sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra generated by X in \(\mathfrak{g}\). The algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\) is obviously commutative and its reductive part (4.2.2) is zero, for otherwise, by loc. cit., \(\mathfrak{h}\) would contain an element \(\mathrm{Y} \neq 0\) such that \(\mathrm{Y}^{(p)} = \mathrm{Y}\), and consequently (App. II 2.1 and 2.2) G would contain a subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), contrary to the hypothesis. Hence \(\mathfrak{h}\), and consequently \(\mathfrak{g}\), is a unipotent \(p\)-Lie algebra.
v) \(\Rightarrow\) i). This is the least trivial implication of 4.2.1.

a) Case where G is of height 1 (Exp. VIIA 4.1.3). Since G is radicial, it is affine, hence isomorphic to an algebraic subgroup of a linear group \(\mathrm{GL}(\mathrm{V})\) (Exp. VIB §11). This embedding identifies \(\mathfrak{g}\) with a sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra of \(\operatorname{End}(\mathrm{V})\), the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power of X in \(\mathfrak{g}\) coinciding with the \(p^{\text{th}}\) power of the endomorphism X (Exp. VIIA 6.4.4). Since \(\mathfrak{g}\) is unipotent by hypothesis, \(\mathfrak{g}\) is thus an algebra of nilpotent endomorphisms of V and by Engel's theorem (Bourbaki, Groupes et algèbres de Lie, chap. I §4 th. 1) is a Lie subalgebra of the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\) of the group of strictly upper triangular matrices \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\) with respect to a suitable basis of V. Since G is of height 1, we then deduce from App. II 2.2 that G itself is an algebraic subgroup of \(\operatorname{Trigstr}(n)\), hence is unipotent (3.5 v) \(\Rightarrow\) i)).

b) General case. Let us proceed by induction on the height \(h\) of G∗. The case \(h = 1\) has just been treated. Suppose \(h > 1\), set \(\mathrm{G}' = {}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G}\) and \(\mathrm{G}'' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}'\). The group \(\mathrm{G}'\) is of height 1 and \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}' = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\) is unipotent, so \(\mathrm{G}'\) is unipotent by a). To show that G is unipotent, it therefore suffices to prove that \(\mathrm{G}''\) is unipotent (2.2). But \(\mathrm{G}''\) is of height \(h - 1\), so, by the induction hypothesis, it suffices to show that \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}''\) is unipotent. Since iv) \(\Rightarrow\) v), it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{G}''_{\bar{k}}\) contains no groups isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\). So let there be a subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}''_{\bar{k}}\) isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), and let H be its inverse image in \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\). The group \(\mathrm{G}'\) being unipotent, we prove in §5 that the extension: \[ e \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}'_{\bar{k}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \boldsymbol{\mu}_p \longrightarrow e \] is necessarily trivial (the proof given of this fact is independent of the results of the present section). In short, the group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) lifts into H, but being of height 1 it is necessarily contained in \(\mathrm{G}'_{\bar{k}} = {}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\), whence a contradiction, \(\mathrm{G}'\) being unipotent.
4.3. Connected affine groups in characteristic \(p > 0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3.1

Let G be a connected affine algebraic group over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p > 0\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

i) G is unipotent.

ii) G possesses a composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) and \(\mathbb{G}_a\) (taken in that order).

iii) G admits a characteristic composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r\) and \((\mathbb{G}_a)^s\) (taken in that order).

iv) \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) contains no subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\).

v) \(\mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\) is unipotent (3.6).

vi) \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent, and the reductive part of the centre of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (4.2.2) is trivial.

vi bis) \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent, and every subgroup of multiplicative type of the identity component of the centre of G is zero.

vi ter) G is nilpotent, and every subgroup of multiplicative type of the identity component of the centre of G is zero.
Proof. It is clear that iii) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i). To establish i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii), we shall need the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.3.2

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p > 0\), \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), \(k'\) a radicial extension of \(k\) such that \((k')^{p^n}\) is contained in \(k\); for every \(k\)-prescheme X (resp. every \(k'\)-prescheme \(\mathrm{X}'\)) denote by \(\mathrm{X}^{(p^n)}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{X}'_\varphi\)) the \(k\)-prescheme deduced from X (resp. \(\mathrm{X}'\)) by the base change: \[ \mathrm{F}^n : k \longrightarrow k, \quad x \mapsto x^{p^n}, \qquad (\text{resp. } \varphi : k' \longrightarrow k, \quad x' \mapsto x'^{p^n}). \] Then, for every \(k\)-prescheme X, there exists a functorial isomorphism: \[ (\mathrm{X}_{k'})_\varphi \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{X}^{(p^n)}. \] Consequently, if X and Y are two \(k\)-preschemes such that there exists a \(k'\)-isomorphism \(u' : \mathrm{X}_{k'} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Y}_{k'}\), then there exists a \(k\)-isomorphism \(v : \mathrm{X}^{(p^n)} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Y}^{(p^n)}\). If moreover X and Y are equipped with structures of \(k\)-group preschemes and if \(u'\) is a \(k'\)-homomorphism, then \(v\) is a \(k\)-homomorphism.
The lemma follows simply from the transitivity of base changes and the fact that the composed morphism \(k \to k' \xrightarrow{\varphi} k\) is equal to \(\mathrm{F}^n\).
Continuation of the proof of 4.3.1.

i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii). Let us proceed by induction on \(\dim \mathrm{G}\). If \(\dim \mathrm{G} = 0\), since G is connected, it is radicial and we apply 3.9 i). If \(\dim \mathrm{G} > 0\), there exists an integer \(m \geqslant 0\) such that the quotient \(\mathrm{G}/{}_{\mathrm{F}^m}\mathrm{G}\) is a smooth group (App. II 3.1), obviously connected and nonzero. Applying 4.1.1 i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii) to the latter, we see that there exists an algebraic subgroup \(\mathrm{G}'\) of G which is characteristic and such that the quotient \(\mathrm{G}'' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}'\) is a form of \(\mathbb{G}_a^r\) (\(r > 0\)). By 4.1.5, if K is a perfect closure of \(k\), we have \(\mathrm{G}''_\mathrm{K} \xrightarrow{\sim} (\mathbb{G}_{a, \mathrm{K}})^r\). Since \(\mathrm{G}''\) is of finite type over \(k\), there exists a finite radicial extension \(k'\) of \(k\) such that \(\mathrm{G}''_{k'} \xrightarrow{\sim} (\mathbb{G}_{a, k'})^r\) (Exp. VIB §10). Let \(n > 0\) be such that \((k')^{p^n} \subset k\). Keeping the notation of 4.3.2, we deduce that there exists a \(k\)-isomorphism of algebraic groups: \[ (\mathbb{G}_{a, k})^r \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }_\varphi (\mathbb{G}_{a, k'})^r \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (\mathrm{G}'')^{(p^n)}. \] Consider then the Frobenius homomorphism relative to \(\mathrm{G}''\) (Exp. VIIA §4) \[ \mathrm{F}^n : \mathrm{G}'' \longrightarrow (\mathrm{G}'')^{(p^n)}. \] Since \(\mathrm{G}''\) (hence also \((\mathrm{G}'')^{(p^n)}\)) is smooth over \(k\), and \(\mathrm{F}^n\) is radicial, \(\mathrm{F}^n\) is an epimorphism for the fpqc topology, so that \((\mathrm{G}'')^{(p^n)}\) identifies with \(\mathrm{G}''/{}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G}'')\). Finally we have shown that \(\mathrm{G}''/{}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G}'')\) is isomorphic, as an algebraic group, to \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\). The inverse image \(\mathrm{G}'_n\) of \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G}'')\) in G is a connected, characteristic subgroup of G, of dimension strictly less than that of G, to which we can apply the induction hypothesis.
i) \(\Rightarrow\) iv) by 2.4 i).

iv) \(\Rightarrow\) i). Consider G as an extension of a smooth connected group \(\mathrm{G}''\) by a radicial group \(\mathrm{G}'\) (App. II 3.1). The group \(\mathrm{G}'\) is unipotent (4.2.1 iv) \(\Leftrightarrow\) i)). It suffices to see that \(\mathrm{G}''\) is unipotent, and for this it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{G}''_{\bar{k}}\) contains no subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) (4.1.1 i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) iv)). Now if \(\mathrm{G}''_{\bar{k}}\) contained a subgroup \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), it would lift into \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\), by the already used result (5.1) proved in §5, whence a contradiction with iv).
i) \(\Rightarrow\) v) by 3.7.

v) \(\Rightarrow\) vi). Indeed, since \((\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X})^{p^r} = \operatorname{ad}(\mathrm{X}^{(p^r)})\) (VIIA 5.2), \(\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}\) is nilpotent if \(\mathfrak{g}\) is unipotent, so \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent by Engel's theorem (Bourbaki, Groupes et algèbres de Lie chap. I §4). On the other hand, if \(\mathfrak{g}\) is unipotent, the same obviously holds for its centre, whose reductive part is then trivial (4.2.2).

vi) \(\Rightarrow\) iv). Indeed, if \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) contains a subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), there exists an element \(\mathrm{X} \neq 0\) of its Lie algebra such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}\) (App. II 2.1), hence \(\mathrm{X}^{(p^r)} = \mathrm{X}\) for every \(r > 0\). Since \(\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}\) is nilpotent because \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent, and \((\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X})^{p^r} = \operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}^{(p^r)}\), necessarily \(\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X} = 0\), so X belongs to the reductive part of the centre of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\bar{k}}\), whence a contradiction with vi).
i) \(\Rightarrow\) vi ter) follows from 2.4 i) and 3.5 i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii).

vi ter) \(\Rightarrow\) vi bis). Indeed, if G is nilpotent, the same holds for its subgroup \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G}\). On the other hand it follows from App. II 2.2 that \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G}\) is nilpotent if and only if \(\operatorname{Lie} {}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\) (Exp. VIIA) is nilpotent.

vi bis) \(\Rightarrow\) vi). Let Z be the identity component of the centre of G and let \(\mathfrak{r}\) be the reductive component of the centre of \(\mathfrak{g}\). We must show that \(\mathfrak{r} = 0\). Now it is immediate that \(\mathfrak{r}\) is a characteristic sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra of \(\mathfrak{g} = \operatorname{Lie} {}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G}\) (i.e. stable under the functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{p\text{-Lie}}(\mathfrak{g})\)); hence \(\mathfrak{r}\) is the Lie algebra of a characteristic radicial subgroup R of \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G}\) (App. II 2.2). On the other hand, it follows from the last assertion contained in 4.2.2 and from App. II 2.1 that R is a form of \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_p)^r\). The group R being characteristic in \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G}\), which is itself a characteristic subgroup of G (App. II 1), R is a fortiori normal in G, hence is central, G being connected (Exp. IX 5.5). Hence by hypothesis vi bis) R is zero, and the same therefore holds for \(\mathfrak{r}\).
4.4. Étale groups.
The following proposition is an easy consequence of the Sylow theorems and the structure of finite \(q\)-groups (cf. J.-P. Serre, Corps locaux, chap. IX §1).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4.1

Let G be a finite étale algebraic group defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Then for G to be unipotent, it is necessary and sufficient that for every prime number \(q\) distinct from the characteristic \(p\) of \(k\), G contain no subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_q\).
4.5. Abelian varieties.
Let G be an abelian variety defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:
i) G is unipotent.
ii) \(\mathrm{G} = 0\).
iii) There exists an integer \(n\), prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(k\), such that G contains no subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\).

Indeed, if G is an abelian variety of dimension \(d\), it is known (cf. S. Lang, Abelian varieties, chap. IV §3. th. 6.) that the group \({}_n\mathrm{G}(k)\) (\(n\) an integer prime to \(p\)) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^{2d}\), hence is isomorphic to \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)^{2d}\). Whence ii) \(\Rightarrow\) iii), and ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i) \(\Rightarrow\) iii) are evident.
4.6. General case.

If G and H are two algebraic groups defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\), we shall denote by \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H})\) the property: "there exists no algebraic subgroup of G isomorphic to H". We then obtain the following characterizations of unipotent groups:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.6.1

Let G be an algebraic group defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(p\). Then:

i) If G is smooth, affine and connected:
G is unipotent \(\Longleftrightarrow\) \(\exists n > 1\) such that \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds \(\Longleftrightarrow\) \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \mathbb{G}_m)\) holds.

ii) If G is smooth and connected:
G is unipotent \(\Longleftrightarrow\) \(\exists n\) prime to \(p\) such that \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds.

iii) If G is smooth:
G is unipotent \(\Longleftrightarrow\) for every prime number \(n \neq p\), \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds.

iv) G affine connected and \(p > 0\): G unipotent \(\Longleftrightarrow\) \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_p)\) holds.

v) G connected and \(p > 0\):
G unipotent \(\Longleftrightarrow\) \(\exists n\) prime to \(p\) such that \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds and \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_p)\) holds.

vi) G an arbitrary algebraic group:
G is unipotent \(\Longleftrightarrow\) for every prime number \(n\), \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds.
Proof. i) follows from 4.1.1, and iv) from 4.3.1. We shall prove vi); ii), iii), v) are proved in an analogous way and are left to the reader.

So let G be an algebraic group. If G is unipotent, \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds for every \(n > 1\) (2.4 i)). Conversely, suppose \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds for every prime number \(n\) and let us show that G is unipotent. Let \(\mathrm{G}^0\) be the identity component of G. If \(\mathrm{G}^0\) is smooth, it follows from a classical theorem of Chevalley∗ that \(\mathrm{G}^0\) is an extension of an abelian variety A by an affine, connected, smooth group L. If G is not smooth, which presupposes \(p > 0\) (Exp. VIB 1.6.1), there exists an integer \(n > 0\) such that \(\mathrm{G}'' = \mathrm{G}^0/{}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G})\) is smooth (App. II 3.1). Hence \(\mathrm{G}''\) is an extension of an abelian variety A by a smooth connected linear group \(\mathrm{L}''\). Denote by L the inverse image of \(\mathrm{L}''\) in \(\mathrm{G}^0\), which is again affine and connected, since \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G})\) is radicial. In all cases, G thus possesses a composition series: \[ 0 \subset \mathrm{L} \subset \mathrm{G}^0 \subset \mathrm{G} \] such that L is affine and connected, \(\mathrm{G}^0/\mathrm{L} = \mathrm{A}\) is an abelian variety, and \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}^0\) an étale group.

If \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds, a fortiori \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{L}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) holds, so L is unipotent (4.1.1 and 4.3.1). If A is not zero, there exist a prime number \(n\) and a subgroup of A isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) (4.5); by 5.1 below, this subgroup lifts into G, which contradicts the hypothesis \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\); hence \(\mathrm{A} = 0\). Finally, if \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}^0\) is not unipotent, there exist an integer \(q\) and a subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}^0\) isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_q\) (4.4.1). We deduce as above that \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{G}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_q)\) does not hold; hence \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}^0\) is unipotent, and consequently so is G.

footnotes.html: i.e. the largest integer such that \(\mathrm{F}^h = \operatorname{id}_\mathrm{G}\), cf. App. II 1.

pages.title: 5. Extension of a group of multiplicative type by a unipotent group

blocks.html: 5. Extension of a group of multiplicative type by a unipotent group
5.1. Statement of the theorem.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1.0

Let \(k\) be a field, G a \(k\)-algebraic group. Following the terminology introduced by Rosenlicht (Questions of rationality for solvable algebraic groups over non perfect fields, Annali di Math. 61 (1963)), we shall say that G is "\(k\)-solvable" if it satisfies the following equivalent conditions:

i) G possesses a composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\).

ii) G possesses a characteristic composition series \((\mathrm{G}_i)\) such that the successive quotients \(\mathrm{G}_i/\mathrm{G}_{i+1}\) are commutative and possess a composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\).
The fact that i) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) is proved in loc. cit. In fact, one can take for the composition series \((\mathrm{G}_i)\) the composition series introduced in the proof of 3.9 ii).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1.1

Let \(k\) be a field, U a unipotent \(k\)-algebraic group, H a \(k\)-group of multiplicative type, E a \(k\)-algebraic group which is an extension of H by U, so that we have the exact sequence: \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathrm{E} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow 1. \] Then:

i) The extension E is trivial in each of the following cases:

a) \(k\) is algebraically closed.
b) \(k\) is perfect and one of the groups U or H is connected.
c) U is \(k\)-solvable.
d) U is smooth and H is connected.

ii) If \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) are two liftings of H into E, then \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) in each of the following cases:

a) \(k\) is algebraically closed and H is smooth.
b) \(k\) is algebraically closed and U is smooth.

(We shall point out in the course of the proof other cases where the conclusion of ii) is true without assuming \(k\) algebraically closed.)

If U is an algebraic group (resp. a commutative algebraic group) defined over a field \(k\), we denote by \(\mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathrm{U})\) (resp. \(\mathrm{H}^i(k, \mathrm{U})\), \(i \geqslant 0\)) the pointed set (resp. the \(i^{\text{th}}\) group) of Galois cohomology of \(k\) with values in U (cf. J.-P. Serre, Cohomologie Galoisienne, Lecture Notes Maths. n°5, Springer).

If S is a prescheme, H a commutative S-group prescheme, G an S-group prescheme acting on H, we denote by \(\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H})\)7 the \(i^{\text{th}}\) Hochschild cohomology group of G with values in H (App. I 1).
To prove 5.1.1, we shall proceed in several steps.
5.2. Proof of 5.1.1 i) and ii) in the case U smooth and H étale.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.1

With the notation of 5.1.1, if H is étale, the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{H}\) possesses a section \(s : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{E}\), defined over \(k\), in the following cases:

a) \(k\) is algebraically closed.

b) \(k\) is perfect and U is smooth and connected.

c) U is "\(k\)-solvable".

a) follows simply from the fact that \(\mathrm{E}(k) \to \mathrm{H}(k)\) is surjective. We have b) \(\Rightarrow\) c) by 4.1.2 b) and 2.3 (bis). It therefore suffices to treat case c). Now let \(x\) be a point of H; then \(x\) is a subset of H that is both open and closed, and the induced subscheme is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{K}\), where K is a finite separable extension of \(k\). Let X be the inverse image in E of the scheme \(x\). The K-scheme X is a principal homogeneous K-space under the group \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\). But \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) possesses a composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_a)_\mathrm{K}\), hence \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}) = 0\) (J.-P. Serre, Cohomologie Galoisienne, chap. III, prop. 6) and consequently X is trivial, hence possesses a rational point over K. We thus obtain a \(k\)-section of \(\mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{H}\) over \(x\), for every point \(x\) of H, whence the existence of a section \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{E}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.3

8 With the notation of 5.1.1, suppose H is étale. Then:

i) The extension E is trivial in each of the following cases:

a) U is commutative and \(\mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{H}\) possesses a section.
b) \(k\) is algebraically closed.
c) \(k\) is perfect and U is connected.
d) U is "\(k\)-solvable".

Moreover, in each of the above cases, two liftings \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) of H into E are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\).

ii) Let R be the \(k\)-functor \((\mathbf{Sch}/k)^\circ \to \mathbf{Ens}\) such that, for every \(k\)-prescheme S, \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{S})\) is the set of liftings of \(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{S}\) into \(\mathrm{E}_\mathrm{S}\). Then if U is commutative, R is representable by a nonempty affine \(k\)-scheme. The group U acts on R by inner automorphisms, and this action makes R a homogeneous space under U (for the fpqc topology (cf. Exp. IV)).
Proof of i). We shall reduce cases b), c) and d) to case a).

Case b) Since U possesses a characteristic composition series with commutative successive quotients (3.5 i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) iv)), we reduce immediately to the case where U is commutative. Moreover \(\mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{H}\) possesses a section by 5.2.1 a) and we are reduced to case a).
Case d) We proceed in the same way, using 5.1.0 ii) and 5.2.1 c).

Case c) Let \(\mathrm{E}_{\text{réd}}\) be the reduced subgroup associated with E. Since H is smooth, \(\mathrm{E}_{\text{réd}}\) is an extension of H by \(\mathrm{U}' = \mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{E}_{\text{réd}}\), and we may replace E by \(\mathrm{E}_{\text{réd}}\). But then U, hence also \(\mathrm{U}'\), is connected, and it is clear that \(\mathrm{U}'\) is the identity component of \(\mathrm{E}_{\text{réd}}\), hence is smooth. But then \(\mathrm{U}'\) is smooth and connected, \(k\) is perfect, so \(\mathrm{U}'\) is \(k\)-solvable (4.1.4 b)) and we are reduced to case d).

The preceding reductions show that in cases b), c) and d) we may assume that U possesses a characteristic composition series \((\mathrm{U}_i)\) such that \(\mathrm{U}_i/\mathrm{U}_{i+1}\) is commutative and such that the maps \(\mathrm{U}_i(k) \to (\mathrm{U}_i/\mathrm{U}_{i+1})(k)\) are surjective (in cases c) and d), this last point comes from \(\mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathbb{G}_a) = 0\)). An immediate dévissage shows that it suffices to prove the conjugacy of two liftings \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) in E when U is commutative. In short, it suffices to prove i) a). In that case, the triviality of the extension E is guaranteed if \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{U}) = 0\) (App. I 3.1) and the conjugacy of \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) is guaranteed if \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{U}) = 0\). Now we have the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.4

Let S be a prescheme, U a commutative S-group prescheme, H an étale, finite S-group prescheme of rank \(n\), acting on U. Then the groups \(\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{U})\) (\(i > 0\)) are killed by \(n\) in the following two cases:

a) H is a constant S-group (Exp. I 4.1).

b) S is the spectrum of a field.

Proof of a). The group H is by hypothesis the constant group associated with an ordinary group \(\{\mathrm{H}\}\) of order \(n\). It is clear9 that \(\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{U})\) is isomorphic to the \(i^{\text{th}}\) cohomology group \(\mathrm{H}^i(\{\mathrm{H}\}, \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}))\) of the group \(\{\mathrm{H}\}\) with values in the ordinary group \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S})\), and it is classical that these groups are killed by \(n\) (J.-P. Serre, Corps locaux, Chap. VIII, prop. 4 cor. 1).

Proof of b). Let \(x \in \mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{U})\) (\(i > 0\)), represented by an \(i\)-cocycle \(f : \mathrm{H}^{(i)} \to \mathrm{U}\) (where \(\mathrm{H}^{(i)}\) denotes the product, over \(k\), of \(i\) copies of H). If K is a finite Galois extension of \(k\) which splits H, it follows from a) that \(nf_\mathrm{K}\) is a coboundary. More precisely, an easy computation shows that if we define the K-morphism \(\mathrm{F}_\mathrm{K} : \mathrm{H}_\mathrm{K}^{(i-1)} \to \mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) by the formula: \[ \mathrm{F}_\mathrm{K}(h_1, \ldots, h_{i-1}) = \sum_{h \in \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{K})} f_\mathrm{K}(h_1, \ldots, h_{i-1}, h), \] we have, up to sign: \[ d(\mathrm{F}_\mathrm{K}) = nf_\mathrm{K} \qquad (d \text{ the coboundary operator}). \] Now an immediate Galois argument shows that \(\mathrm{F}_\mathrm{K}\) comes from a \(k\)-morphism \(\mathrm{F} : \mathrm{H}^{(i-1)} \to \mathrm{U}\), and consequently we have \(d(\mathrm{F}) = nf\).

blocks.label: 5.2.4 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2.4 bis

With the notation of 5.2.4, suppose moreover that U is flat and of finite presentation over S, with unipotent fibres, and that \(n\) is prime to the residual characteristics of S. Then, in cases a) and b) above, we have \(\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{U}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\).

It suffices to show that raising to the \(n^{\text{th}}\) power in U is an isomorphism, for this will imply that multiplication by \(n\) in \(\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{U})\) is at once an isomorphism and the zero morphism, hence \(\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{U}) = 0\). Now, under the hypotheses made on U, it suffices to check that raising to the \(n^{\text{th}}\) power is an isomorphism on the fibres of U (EGA IV 17.9.5), which reduces us to the case where S is the spectrum of a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p\). Since \((n, p) = 1\), raising to the \(n^{\text{th}}\) power in U is an étale morphism (Exp. VII), and it is a monomorphism (2.4 i)), hence an open immersion (EGA IV 17.9.1).
