This already proves that its restriction to the identity component \(\mathrm{U}^0\) is an isomorphism; since \(\mathrm{U}/\mathrm{U}^0\) is a finite \(p\)-group, we are done.
This completes the proof of 5.2.3 i) a), since H, being an étale group of multiplicative type, has order prime to \(p\).

Proof of 5.2.3 ii). It is clear that R is a sheaf for the fpqc topology. Moreover, taking into account the descent of affine schemes, the assertions of 5.2.3 ii) are local for the fpqc topology. We may therefore assume \(k\) algebraically closed. The group H is then split and the extension E is trivial (i b)); let \(\mathrm{H}'\) be a lifting of H into E. For every \(k\)-prescheme S and every lifting \(\mathrm{H}''\) of \(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{S}\) into \(\mathrm{E}_\mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm{H}'_\mathrm{S}\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S})\), since \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{U}_\mathrm{S}) = 0\) (5.2.4 bis). Let then \(\mathrm{U}^{\mathrm{H}'}\) be the sheaf of invariants of U under \(\mathrm{H}'\), which is representable by an algebraic subgroup of U (Exp. VIII 6.5 d)). It follows from the preceding remarks that the \(k\)-morphism: \[ \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}, \qquad u \mapsto \operatorname{int}(u)\mathrm{H}' \quad (u \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S})) \] defines a \(k\)-isomorphism \(\mathrm{U}/\mathrm{U}^{\mathrm{H}'} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{R}\). This proves the representability of R and the fact that R is affine (2.1).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.2.5

One can show that the assertions of 5.2.3 ii) are still true when U is not commutative, but we shall not need this to prove 5.1.1.
5.3. Study of the case H smooth.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.3.1

The assertions contained in 5.2.3 i) remain true when the hypothesis "H étale" is replaced by "H smooth".
Proceeding as in the proof of 5.2.3 i), we reduce to the case where moreover U is commutative.

Let \(\mathbb{N}'\) be the set of integers \(> 0\) prime to \(p\), ordered by the divisibility relation. For every \(n \in \mathbb{N}'\), \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is an étale group and the family of the \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) (\(n \in \mathbb{N}'\)) is schematically dense in H, since H is smooth (Exp. IX 4.10). Denote by \(\mathrm{E}_n\) the inverse image of \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) in E, so that \(\mathrm{E}_n\) is an extension of \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) by U, and finally let \(\mathrm{R}_n\) be the \(k\)-functor of liftings of \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) into \(\mathrm{E}_n\) (cf. 5.2.3 ii)). If \(n\) divides \(m\), it is clear that we have a natural \(k\)-morphism \(\mathrm{R}_m \to \mathrm{R}_n\), so that the \(\mathrm{R}_n\) form a projective system of \(k\)-functors. Since \(\mathrm{R}_n\) is representable by a nonempty affine \(k\)-scheme (5.2.3 ii)), and since a filtered inductive limit of nonzero rings is nonzero, the functor \(\mathrm{R} = \varprojlim \mathrm{R}_n\) is representable by a nonempty affine \(k\)-scheme (EGA IV 8 and 1.9.1). There therefore exist an extension K of \(k\) and a point \(u \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{K})\). The image \(u_n\) of \(u\) in \(\mathrm{R}_n(\mathrm{K})\) corresponds to a lifting \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) of \(({}_n\mathrm{H})_\mathrm{K}\) into \((\mathrm{E}_n)_\mathrm{K}\). By construction, \(\mathrm{H}'_n = ({}_n\mathrm{H}'_m)_\mathrm{K}\) if \(n\) divides \(m\). Set \(\mathrm{U}_n = (\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K})^{\mathrm{H}'_n}\). The choice of \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) allows us to identify \((\mathrm{R}_n)_\mathrm{K}\) with \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}/\mathrm{U}_n\). But the family of the \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) is increasing and filtered, so the family of the \(\mathrm{U}_n\) is decreasing and filtered, and consequently is stationary for \(n\) large enough (\(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) is noetherian). It follows that the family of the \((\mathrm{R}_n)_\mathrm{K}\) is stationary, and consequently so is the family of the \(\mathrm{R}_n\). In short, we have \(\mathrm{R}_n = \mathrm{R}\) for \(n\) large enough.

Under the hypotheses made, it follows from 5.2.3 i) that \(\mathrm{R}_n(k)\) is not empty. One can therefore find a coherent system of liftings \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) of \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) for \(n \in \mathbb{N}'\). Denote by \(\mathrm{H}'\) the smallest closed subscheme of E dominating \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) for every \(n\) (Exp. VIB §7). The reasoning carried out in Exp. XV 4.6 shows that \(\mathrm{H}'\) is a smooth commutative algebraic group whose formation commutes with extension of the base field. To show that \(\mathrm{H}'\) is a lifting of H into E, we may therefore assume \(k\) algebraically closed. By BIBLE 4 th. 4, \(\mathrm{H}'\) is then the direct product of a (smooth) group of multiplicative type M and a unipotent group V. The groups \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) are then necessarily contained in M (2.4), and given the definition of \(\mathrm{H}'\) this implies \(\mathrm{H}' = \mathrm{M}\). Hence \(\mathrm{H}'\) is of multiplicative type and consequently \(\mathrm{H}' \cap \mathrm{U} = 0\). The morphism \(\mathrm{H}' \to \mathrm{H}\) is therefore a monomorphism; moreover it follows from the density theorem (Exp. IX 4.10) that it is an epimorphism, so it is indeed an isomorphism.

Now let \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) be two liftings of H into E. For every \(n \in \mathbb{N}'\), denote by \(\mathrm{T}_n = \underline{\operatorname{Transp}}({}_n\mathrm{H}', {}_n\mathrm{H}'')\) the transporter of \({}_n\mathrm{H}'\) into \({}_n\mathrm{H}''\), which is representable by a closed subscheme of U (Exp. VIII 6.5 e)). The \(\mathrm{T}_n\) form a decreasing filtered family of closed subschemes of U, nonempty by 5.2.3 i) a). Let T be the stationary value. Under the hypotheses of 5.2.3 i), \(\mathrm{T}_n(k)\) is not empty. There therefore exists an element \(u\) of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) such that \({}_n\mathrm{H}'' = \operatorname{int}(u){}_n\mathrm{H}'\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}'\). But then \(\mathrm{H}'' = \operatorname{int}(u)\mathrm{H}'\) (Exp. IX 4.8 b)).
5.4. Study of the case U radicial.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.4.1

If \(k\) is a perfect field of characteristic \(p > 0\), and if U is radicial, the extension E of 5.1.1 is trivial.
Using a characteristic composition series of U, we may restrict to the case where U is equal to \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r\) (3.9).

It follows from App. II 2.2 and 2.1 that we have isomorphisms of \(k\)-functors: \[ \underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{k\text{-gr}}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{p\text{-Lie}}(\operatorname{Lie}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{GL}(\operatorname{Lie}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r). \] Consider then a \(k\)-vector space V of rank \(r\), the vector \(k\)-group scheme \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{V})\) (Exp. I 4.6), and identify \((\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)^r\) with \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{V}\). The preceding remarks then show that the action of H on \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{V}\), defined by the extension E, comes from a linear representation \(v\) of H in V. Consider then the exact sequence: \[ (\ast) \quad 0 \to {}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{V} \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{F}\ } \mathrm{V}^{(p)} \to 0, \] where F is the Frobenius morphism. Then \((\ast)\) is an exact sequence of H-groups, provided that H is made to act on the factor \(\mathrm{V}^{(p)}\) via the linear representation \(\mathrm{H} \xrightarrow{v} \mathrm{GL}(\mathrm{V}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} \mathrm{GL}(\mathrm{V}^{(p)})\).

Since the field \(k\) is perfect, the morphism \(\mathrm{F} : \mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{V}^{(p)}\) induces a surjective map \(\mathrm{V}(k) \to \mathrm{V}^{(p)}(k)\). It then follows from App. I 2.1 that the exact sequence \((\ast)\) defines an exact sequence: \[ (\ast\ast) \quad \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{V}^{(p)}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{H}, {}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{V}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{V}). \] Let us show that \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{V}) = 0\). So let \(\mathrm{E}'\) be an algebraic group which is an extension of H by V: \[ 1 \to \mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{E}' \xrightarrow{\ h\ } \mathrm{H} \to 1. \] The scheme \(\mathrm{E}'\) is a torsor with base H and group \(\mathbb{G}_a^r\), hence defines an element of \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{H}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{H}^r)\) (in the sense of cohomology of coherent sheaves). Since H is affine, we have \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{H}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{H}^r) = 0\) (EGA III §1). This says that \(\mathrm{E}' \to \mathrm{H}\) possesses a section. Consequently, the group \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{V})\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{V})\) (App. I 3.1). Now \(\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{V}) = \mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{V})) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (Exp. IX 3.1). We then conclude, by the exact sequence \((\ast\ast)\), that \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{H}, {}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{V}) = 0\), hence that E is a trivial extension.
5.5. Proof of 5.1.1 i).

If \(k\) is of characteristic 0, U is \(k\)-solvable (4.1.3) and H is smooth; the fact that E is a trivial extension therefore follows from 5.3.1 and 5.2.3 d). One proves in the same way that two liftings of H into E are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\).
From now on, we therefore assume that \(k\) is a field of characteristic \(p > 0\).
Proof of i) b): Case \(k\) perfect, U connected.

We shall reduce to the case where U is radicial. To this end, note that \(k\) being perfect, \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}}\) is smooth; let \(\mathrm{E}'\) be its inverse image in E. It then follows from 5.3.1 and 5.2.3 i) c) that the extension: \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathrm{E}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}} \longrightarrow 1 \] is trivial. Let \(\mathrm{H}_1\) be a lifting of \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}}\) into E. By App. II 3.1, there exists an integer \(n > 0\) such that \(\mathrm{E}^{(n)} = \mathrm{E}/{}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{E})\) is smooth; let \(\mathrm{E}''\) be the algebraic subgroup of E generated by \(\mathrm{H}_1\) and \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{E})\) (i.e. the inverse image in E of the image of \(\mathrm{H}_1\) in \(\mathrm{E}^{(n)}\)). Denote by \(\mathrm{H}''\) the image of \(\mathrm{E}''\) in H. Then I claim that \(\mathrm{H}'' = \mathrm{H}\). Indeed, denote by R the image of \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{E})\) in H, so that \(\mathrm{H}''\) is generated by R and \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}}\). The group \(\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{R}\) is a quotient of \(\mathrm{E}^{(n)}\), hence is smooth; consequently the canonical morphism \[ \mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}/\mathrm{R} \] is an epimorphism, so H is generated by R and \(\mathrm{H}_{\text{réd}}\), hence is equal to \(\mathrm{H}''\). We thus obtain an exact sequence: \[ (\dagger) \quad 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{U}'' = \mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{E}'' \longrightarrow \mathrm{E}'' \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow 1. \] But \(\mathrm{E}''\) has the same underlying space as \(\mathrm{H}_1\), so \(\mathrm{U}''\) is radicial. Moreover, it is clear that it suffices to prove that the extension \((\dagger)\) is trivial, which follows from 5.4.1.
Proof of i) b): Case \(k\) perfect, H connected.

The group U is an extension of an étale group by its identity component \(\mathrm{U}^0\). The case U connected having just been treated, it suffices to examine the case U étale. We then have the more precise lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.1

With the notation of 5.1.1, suppose moreover that U is étale. Then:

i) If H is connected, there exists a unique lifting of H into E, namely the identity component \(\mathrm{E}^0\) of E.

ii) If \(k\) is algebraically closed, E is trivial, and two liftings of H into E are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\).

i) The formation of the identity component commuting with extension of the base field, we may restrict to \(k\) algebraically closed. If H is a torus, E is trivial (5.3.1 and 5.2.3 i b)), and it is clear that \(\mathrm{E}^0\) is the unique lifting of H. This already proves that in the general case, \(\mathrm{E}^0 \cap \mathrm{U}\) is radicial; since on the other hand it is étale (U being étale), it is zero. The morphism \(\mathrm{E}^0 \to \mathrm{H}\) is therefore a monomorphism, flat (since \(\mathrm{E}^0\) is open in E) and surjective (H is connected), hence is an isomorphism. If now \(\mathrm{H}'\) is another lifting of H, \(\mathrm{H}'\) is connected, hence contained in \(\mathrm{E}^0\), and consequently is equal to \(\mathrm{E}^0\).

ii) Let \(\mathrm{H}^0\) be the identity component of H. By i), \(\mathrm{E}^0\) is the unique lifting of \(\mathrm{H}^0\) into E. Set \(\mathrm{E}' = \mathrm{E}/\mathrm{E}^0\), \(\mathrm{H}' = \mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}^0\), so that we have the extension: \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathrm{E}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}' \longrightarrow 1. \] \(\mathrm{H}'\) being étale, this extension is trivial (5.2.3 i b)). If \(\mathrm{H}'_1\) is a lifting of \(\mathrm{H}'\) into \(\mathrm{E}'\), and \(\mathrm{H}_1\) its inverse image in E, it is clear that \(\mathrm{H}_1\) lifts H into E. If \(\mathrm{H}_2\) is a second lifting of H into E, it contains \(\mathrm{E}^0\); by 5.2.3 i b), the image of \(\mathrm{H}_2\) in \(\mathrm{E}'\) is conjugate to \(\mathrm{H}'_1\) by an element \(u\) of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\), whence immediately \(\mathrm{H}_2 = \operatorname{int}(u)\mathrm{H}_1\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.5.2

Under the hypotheses of 5.5.1 i), it is easy to see that \(\mathrm{E}^0\) centralizes U.

Proof of 5.1.1 i) a). Using the composition series \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{U}^0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathrm{U}/\mathrm{U}^0 \longrightarrow 1, \] i) a) follows from the combination of i) b) and 5.5.1 ii).
Before proving 5.1.1 c) and d), we shall first establish 5.1.1 ii).
Proof of 5.1.1 ii) a). For want of a satisfactory general statement, we shall describe a certain number of cases where, when H is smooth, two liftings of H into E are conjugate:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.6.1

With the notation of 5.1.1, suppose moreover H smooth, and let \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) be two liftings of H into E. Then \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) in each of the following cases:

a) \(k\) is algebraically closed.

b) U is commutative.

c) \(k\) is perfect and U is connected.

d) U is \(k\)-solvable.

e) The centralizer of \(\mathrm{H}'\) in U is \(k\)-solvable.

f) The group of multiplicative type H is trivialized by a finite Galois extension K of \(k\) of degree prime to \(p\).
Proof. a), b), c), d) follow from 5.3.1.

e) Let Z be the centralizer of \(\mathrm{H}'\) in U, T the transporter of \(\mathrm{H}'\) into \(\mathrm{H}''\). By a), T is not empty, so T is a principal homogeneous space under Z, and the hypothesis made on Z implies that it is trivial.

f) Proceeding as in 5.3.1, we see that it suffices to consider the case H étale. Suppose first H diagonalizable, defined by the ordinary group M, of order \(m\) prime to \(p\). The datum of the two liftings \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) defines a 1-cocycle of M with values in \(\mathrm{U}(k)\), that is, a map \(h\) from M to \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) such that \(h(mn) = h(m) {}^m h(n)\) for every pair \(m, n\) of elements of M. The groups \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) if and only if there exists \(a \in \mathrm{U}(k)\) such that \[ h(m) = a^{-1} ({}^m a). \] Now the abstract group \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) possesses a composition series with successive quotients commutative and killed by a power of \(p\) (we may assume \(p > 0\), in view of 5.6.1 c)). We immediately deduce in this case that \(h\) is a coboundary.

Let us now examine the general case. Denote again by Z the centralizer of \(\mathrm{H}'\) in U and by T the transporter of \(\mathrm{H}'\) into \(\mathrm{H}''\), which is a torsor under Z. By hypothesis, there exists a finite Galois extension K of \(k\), with Galois group G of order prime to \(p\), which trivializes H. By the study made above, \(\mathrm{H}'_\mathrm{K}\) and \(\mathrm{H}''_\mathrm{K}\) are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{K})\), so \(\mathrm{T}_\mathrm{K}\) is trivial. Consequently T is defined by an element of \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{Z})\). For the same reasons as above, the hypothesis made on G implies that \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{Z}) = e\), so T is trivial.
5.7. Proof of 5.1.1 ii) b).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.7.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, separated and smooth over S, H an S-group of multiplicative type acting on G. Then the S-functor \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{G}^\mathrm{H}\) of invariants of G under H is representable by a sub-S-group prescheme of G, closed and smooth over S.

The fact that \(\mathrm{G}^\mathrm{H}\) is representable by a closed sub-group prescheme of G follows from Exp. VIII 6.5 d). Consider then the semidirect product \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{G} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{H}\). The centralizer of H in K is then equal to \(\mathrm{Z} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{H}\). To prove that Z is smooth, we must check that if S is affine, if \(\mathrm{S}_0\) is a closed subscheme of S defined by an ideal of square zero, and if \(u_0\) is an element of \(\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{S}_0)\), then there exists an element \(u\) of \(\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{S})\) lifting \(u_0\). Now since G is smooth over S, there exists an element \(u\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) lifting \(u_0\). Let \(i\) be the canonical immersion of H into K and consider the two S-group morphisms: \[ \mathrm{H} \rightrightarrows \mathrm{K}, \qquad i \quad \text{and} \quad \operatorname{int}(u)i = j. \] Since \(u_0\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{S}_0)\), we have \(i_{\mathrm{S}_0} = j_{\mathrm{S}_0}\). By Exp. IX 3.2, there exists \(v \in \mathrm{K}(\mathrm{S})\) which lifts the unit section of \(\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{S}_0)\) and is such that \[ i = \operatorname{int}(v)j = \operatorname{int}(vu)i. \] Hence \(vu = (u', v')\) belongs to \((\mathrm{Z} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{H})(\mathrm{S})\). Hence \(u'\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{S})\) and lifts \(u_0\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.7.2

Let \(k\) be a field, H a \(k\)-algebraic group, and let \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{H})\) be the following property:

"for every smooth unipotent \(k\)-group U, and for every extension E of H by U, two liftings of H into E are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\)".

Then if H is an algebraic group which is an extension of a group of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{H}''\) by a group of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{H}'\), and if \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{H}')\) and \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{H}'')\) hold, then \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{H})\) holds.

Indeed, let U be a smooth unipotent \(k\)-group, E an extension of H by U, \(\mathrm{H}_1\) and \(\mathrm{H}_2\) two liftings of H into E, \(\mathrm{H}'_1\) and \(\mathrm{H}'_2\) the corresponding liftings of \(\mathrm{H}'\). Since \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{H}')\) holds, there exists \(u \in \mathrm{U}(k)\) such that \(\mathrm{H}'_2 = \operatorname{int}(u)\mathrm{H}'_1\). After possibly replacing \(\mathrm{H}_1\) by \(\operatorname{int}(u)\mathrm{H}_1\), we may assume \(\mathrm{H}'_1 = \mathrm{H}'_2\), which we allow ourselves to denote simply by \(\mathrm{H}'\). Let \(\mathrm{E}' = \underline{\operatorname{Centre}}_\mathrm{E} \mathrm{H}'\), which is equal to \[ \mathrm{U}^{\mathrm{H}'} \cdot \mathrm{H}_1 = \mathrm{U}^{\mathrm{H}'} \cdot \mathrm{H}_2 . \] By 5.6.1, \(\mathrm{U}^{\mathrm{H}'} = \mathrm{U}'\) is smooth. Consider then the extension \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{U}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{E}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow 1. \] By construction \(\mathrm{H}'\) is central in \(\mathrm{E}'\), hence normal. Passing to the quotient, we obtain the exact sequence: \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{U}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{E}'' \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}'' \longrightarrow 1. \] Since \(\mathrm{U}'\) is smooth, and since \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{H}'')\) holds, the two images of \(\mathrm{H}_1\) and \(\mathrm{H}_2\) in \(\mathrm{E}''\) are conjugate by an element \(u\) of \(\mathrm{U}'(k)\); but then \(\mathrm{H}_1 = \operatorname{int}(u)\mathrm{H}_2\).

To prove 5.1.1 ii) b), note then that, \(k\) being algebraically closed, H possesses a composition series whose successive quotients are smooth or isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) when \(p > 0\). By repeated use of 5.7.2, we are reduced to the case where H is smooth or equal to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\). In the first case, it suffices to apply 5.1.1 ii) a). There remains the case \(\mathrm{H} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_p\). Since U is smooth, U possesses a characteristic composition series with successive quotients étale or isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\) (3.9). If U is étale, we apply 5.5.1. There finally remains the case \(\mathrm{H} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{G}_a^r\).

We must show that \(\mathrm{H}^1(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p, \mathrm{U}) = 0\). The method used in 5.4.1 no longer applies here, since \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) does not in general act linearly on \((\mathbb{G}_a)^r\). Let us fix notation: \(\mathrm{H}'\) denotes a lifting of H into E, \(\mathfrak{e} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E}\), \(\mathfrak{u} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{U}\), \(\mathfrak{h} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}\), \(\mathfrak{h}' = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}'\). Let X be a nonzero element of \(\mathfrak{h}\) such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}\) (App. II 2.1) and let \(\mathrm{X}'\) be its lifting into \(\mathfrak{h}'\).

Since \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) is a radicial group of height 1, there is a one-to-one correspondence between the set of liftings of H into E and the set A of the \(\mathrm{Y} \in \mathfrak{e}\) such that \(\mathrm{Y}^{(p)} = \mathrm{Y}\) which project onto X (App. II 2.2). Likewise, if \(\mathrm{Y} \in \mathrm{A}\) corresponds to the lifting \(\mathrm{H}''\) of H into E, and if \(u \in \mathrm{U}(k)\), then \(\operatorname{int}(u)\mathrm{H}'' = \mathrm{H}''\) if and only if \(\operatorname{Ad}(u)\mathrm{X} = \mathrm{Y}\). So let B be the subset of the \(\mathrm{Y} \in \mathfrak{e}\) of the form \(\operatorname{Ad}(u)\mathrm{X}\), where \(u \in \mathrm{U}(k)\). We obviously have \(\mathrm{B} \subset \mathrm{A}\), and everything comes down to showing that \(\mathrm{A} = \mathrm{B}\) if \(k\) is algebraically closed.

a) Study of A. Since \(\mathfrak{u}\) is commutative and normalized by \(\mathfrak{h}\), Jacobson's formula (Exp. VIIA 5.2) gives simply here, for \(u \in \mathfrak{u}\): \[ (\mathrm{X}' + u)^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}'^{(p)} + u^{(p)} + (\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}')^{p-1}(u) = \mathrm{X}' + (\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}')^{p-1}(u), \] so that \(\mathrm{X}' + u \in \mathrm{A} \Leftrightarrow u = (\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}')^{p-1}(u)\).

Now let \(\mathfrak{u} = \coprod_{n \in \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}} \mathfrak{u}_n\) be the canonical decomposition of \(\mathfrak{u}\) under the action of \(\mathrm{H}'\), which can also be written: \[ \mathfrak{u} = \mathfrak{u}_0 \oplus \coprod_{n \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times} \mathfrak{u}_n. \] If \(u \in \mathfrak{u}_0\), we have \(\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}'(u) = 0\). If \(u \in \mathfrak{u}_n\) (\(n \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times\)), we have \((\operatorname{ad} \mathrm{X}')^{p-1}(u) = u\). Finally, \(\mathrm{Y} = \mathrm{X}' + u\) is an element of A if and only if \(u \in \coprod_{n \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times} \mathfrak{u}_n\). Let us note that A is the set of \(k\)-rational points of an irreducible subscheme of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{e})\), of dimension equal to \(\operatorname{rg} \mathfrak{u} - \operatorname{rg} \mathfrak{u}_0 = \operatorname{rg} \mathfrak{u} - \operatorname{rg} \operatorname{Centr}_\mathfrak{u}(\mathrm{X}')\).
b) Study of B. We shall need the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.7.3

(Rosenlicht). Let U be a unipotent algebraic group over a field \(k\) acting on a quasi-affine \(k\)-scheme X. Then the orbit of every point \(x \in \mathrm{X}(k)\) is closed in X (by the orbit of \(x\) we mean the subset of \(\operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{X})\) which is the image of G under the morphism \(g \mapsto g \cdot x\)).

By functoriality, G acts on the affine envelope of X (that is, \(\operatorname{Spec} \Gamma(\mathrm{X}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X})\)), which allows us to assume X affine. We may then assume \(k\) algebraically closed, X reduced and U smooth (note that \(\mathrm{U}_{\text{réd}}\) acts on \(\mathrm{X}_{\text{réd}}\) if \(k\) is perfect). Let Y be the schematic image of G (EGA I 9.5.1) under the morphism \(g \mapsto g \cdot x\), which is a closed reduced subscheme of X on which G acts. It follows easily from EGA IV 1.8.6 that the orbit of \(x\) is an open subset Z of Y, dense in Y. We must show that \(\mathrm{Z} = \operatorname{ens}(\mathrm{Y})\). Let F be the reduced closed subscheme of Y having \(\mathrm{Y} \setminus \mathrm{Z}\) as underlying space. We thus have \(\mathrm{F} = \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{J})\), where J is a nonzero ideal of \(\Gamma(\mathrm{Y}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{Y})\). Since G is smooth, G acts on F, hence on J, and consequently (3.2) \(\mathrm{J}^\mathrm{G} \neq 0\). If \(a\) is a nonzero element of \(\mathrm{J}^\mathrm{G}\), \(a\) is necessarily constant on the orbit Z, hence is constant on Y, Z being dense in Y. But then the ideal J contains \(k\) and \(\mathrm{F} = \varnothing\).

This being so, let us apply the preceding lemma to the group U acting on the affine space \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{e})\) through the adjoint representation. We obtain that the orbit of \(\mathrm{X}'\) is the underlying set of a closed sub-prescheme of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{e})\). Moreover, the stabilizer Z of \(\mathrm{X}'\) is the centralizer of \(\mathrm{X}'\) in U, and we have a closed immersion: \[ \mathrm{U}/\mathrm{Z} \longrightarrow \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{e}). \] Let us note that the orbit of \(\mathrm{X}'\) is the space underlying a closed subscheme of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{e})\) of dimension equal to \(\dim \mathrm{U} - \dim \mathrm{Z}\).

c) End of the proof of 5.1.1 ii) b). When \(k\) is algebraically closed, the canonical map \(\mathrm{U}(k) \to (\mathrm{U}/\mathrm{Z})(k)\) is surjective, so that by point b) above, B is the set of rational points of a closed subscheme of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{e})\) of dimension \(\dim \mathrm{U} - \dim \mathrm{Z}\). Taking point a) into account, to prove that \(\mathrm{A} = \mathrm{B}\) it then suffices to show that we have: \[ \operatorname{rg} \mathfrak{u} - \operatorname{rg} \operatorname{Centr}_\mathfrak{u}(\mathrm{X}') \leqslant \dim \mathrm{U} - \dim \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{X}'). \] Now U being smooth, we have \(\dim \mathrm{U} = \operatorname{rg} \mathfrak{u}\). On the other hand, we have (Exp. II 5.3.3): \[ \dim \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{X}') \leqslant \operatorname{rg} \operatorname{Centr}_\mathfrak{u}(\mathrm{X}'), \] whence the result (note that we in fact have \(\dim \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{X}') = \operatorname{rg} \operatorname{Centr}_\mathfrak{u}(\mathrm{X}')\), which proves again that Z is smooth (5.7.1)).
5.8. End of the proof of 5.1.1 i).
It remains for us to prove i) c) and i) d).
Proof of 5.1.1 i) d) (U smooth, H connected). We shall need the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.8.1

With the notation of 5.1.1, suppose U smooth and H radicial, and let \(\mathrm{H}_1\) be an algebraic subgroup of H which possesses a lifting \(\mathrm{H}'_1\) into E. Then H possesses a lifting \(\mathrm{H}'\) into E which dominates \(\mathrm{H}'_1\).

By induction on the height of \(\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}_1\), we may assume \(\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}_1\) of height 1. Let \(\mathrm{C}' = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{H}'_1)\). I claim that the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{C}' \to \mathrm{H}\) is an epimorphism. To establish this point, we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed; but then the extension E is trivial (5.1.1 i a)); let \(\mathrm{H}''\) be a lifting of H into E, \(\mathrm{H}''_1\) the inverse image of \(\mathrm{H}_1\) in \(\mathrm{H}''\). The groups \(\mathrm{H}'_1\) and \(\mathrm{H}''_1\) are two liftings of \(\mathrm{H}_1\) into E, hence are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\), since \(k\) is algebraically closed and U smooth (5.1.1 ii b)). It is then clear that to prove the assertion about \(\mathrm{C}'\), it suffices to prove it for \(\mathrm{C}'' = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{H}''_1)\). But in that case, \(\mathrm{C}''\) dominates \(\mathrm{H}''\), and the property is clear. Moreover it follows from 5.7.1 that \(\mathrm{C} \cap \mathrm{U}\) is smooth. It is clear that we may replace E by C, hence assume \(\mathrm{H}'_1\) central. But then, after possibly passing to the quotient by \(\mathrm{H}'_1\), we may assume \(\mathrm{H}_1 = 0\) and H of height 1.

Since U is smooth, we have the exact sequence of \(p\)-Lie algebras (App. II 3.2): \[ (\ast) \quad 0 \longrightarrow \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow 0. \] In view of App. II 2.2, saying that E is trivial is equivalent to saying that \((\ast)\) is a trivial extension of \(p\)-Lie algebras. Suppose \(\mathrm{H} eq 0\) (hence \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H} eq 0\)) and suppose we have found a sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}_1\) of \(\mathfrak{h} = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}\) which is nonzero and lifts to a sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}'_1\) of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E}\). By loc. cit., there exist a subgroup \(\mathrm{H}_1\) of H such that \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}_1 = \mathfrak{h}_1\), and a lifting \(\mathrm{H}'_1\) of \(\mathrm{H}_1\) into E such that \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}'_1 = \mathfrak{h}'_1\). Applying again the reduction described above, we are led back to the same problem, with H replaced by \(\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}_1\). Since H is of height 1, \(\operatorname{Lie}(\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}_1) = \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}/\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}_1\) (loc. cit.), \(\operatorname{rg} \operatorname{Lie}(\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}_1) \leqslant \operatorname{rg} \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H} - 1\). In short, proceeding by induction on the rank of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}\), we see that it suffices, when \(\mathfrak{h} eq 0\), to find a nonzero Lie subalgebra of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}\) which lifts into \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E}\). Now we have the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.8.2

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p > 0\), and \(\varphi\) a surjective morphism of \(p\)-\(k\)-Lie algebras of finite rank \(\mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{h}\). Then:

i) If \(\mathfrak{h}_{\bar{k}}\) is reductive (4.2.2) and \(\neq 0\), there exists a reductive Lie subalgebra \(\mathfrak{h}_1\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) whose image in \(\mathfrak{h}\) is nonzero.

ii) If \(k\) is perfect and if \(u\) is a unipotent element of \(\mathfrak{h}\) (i.e. there exists \(n\) such that \(u^{(p^n)} = 0\)), then \(u\) lifts to a unipotent element of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

Take for X a nonzero element of \(\mathfrak{h}\) in case i) and \(u\) in case ii), and let \(\mathrm{X}'\) be a lifting of X into \(\mathfrak{g}\). The sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) generated by \(\mathrm{X}'\) is an abelian \(p\)-Lie algebra (Exp. VII) \(\mathfrak{h}'\).

Case i). It is clear from the description given in 4.2.2 that the reductive part (loc. cit.) of \(\mathfrak{h}'_k\) is already defined over \(k\); denote it by \(\mathfrak{r}'\). I claim that the image of \(\mathfrak{r}'\) in \(\mathfrak{h}\) is nonzero. To establish this point we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed, so that \(\mathfrak{h}' = \mathfrak{r}' \oplus \mathfrak{u}'\) (\(\mathfrak{u}'\) the unipotent part of \(\mathfrak{h}'\)). If the image of \(\mathfrak{r}'\) in \(\mathfrak{h}\) were zero, the image of \(\mathfrak{h}'\) in \(\mathfrak{h}\) would be unipotent, hence would be zero since \(\mathfrak{h}\) is reductive (cf. 2.4 ii)); yet by construction it contains X.
Case ii). We proceed in the same way, exchanging the roles of \(\mathfrak{r}'\) and \(\mathfrak{u}'\).

End of the proof of 5.8.1. Assuming \(\mathrm{H} \neq 0\), by 5.8.2 there exists a nonzero reductive Lie subalgebra \(\mathfrak{h}'_1\) of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E}\). Since \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{U}\) is unipotent (4.3 i)), we necessarily have \((\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{U}) \cap \mathfrak{h}'_1 = 0\), so that \(\mathfrak{h}'_1\) is a lifting of a sub-\(p\)-Lie algebra of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}\).

End of the proof of 5.1.1 i d). By 5.8.1, there exists a family of algebraic subgroups \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) (\(n \in \mathbb{N}\)) of E such that \(\mathrm{H}'_{n+1}\) dominates \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) and such that \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) lifts \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n} \mathrm{H}\). The decreasing sequence of subgroups \(\underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{H}'_n)\) is stationary; let C be the stationary value. The centre Z of C dominates \(\mathrm{H}'_n\) for every \(n\), so the image of Z in H dominates \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{H})\) for every \(n\), and consequently is an open subgroup of H (Exp. VIIA §4), hence is equal to H since H is connected. To prove that E is trivial, we may therefore replace E by Z, hence assume E commutative. We shall then see in 7.2.1 that E contains a maximal subgroup of multiplicative type M, whose formation commutes with extension of the base field. Since \(\mathrm{E}_{\bar{k}}\) is a trivial extension (5.1.1 i a)) and U is unipotent, it is clear that M is the unique lifting of H into E.

Proof of 5.1.1 i) c) (U \(k\)-solvable). Since \((\mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}^0)(k)\) is of order prime to \(p\), it is immediate by duality that there exists an integer \(n\) such that \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) is an étale subgroup and such that the canonical morphism \({}_n\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{H}/\mathrm{H}^0\) is an epimorphism, so that \(\mathrm{H}/{}_n\mathrm{H}\) is connected. By 5.2.3 d), there exists a lifting \(\mathrm{H}'\) of \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) into E. One shows, as at the beginning of the proof of 5.8.1, that \(\mathrm{C} = \underline{\operatorname{Centr}}_\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{H}')\) is a subgroup of E such that \(\mathrm{C} \to \mathrm{H}\) is an epimorphism and such that \(\mathrm{C} \cap \mathrm{U}\) is smooth. Replacing E by C and passing to the quotient by \(\mathrm{H}'\), we are reduced to the case where U is smooth and H connected, that is, to case i) d).
This completes the proof of 5.1.1.
5.9. Counterexamples.

Let us first indicate a procedure for obtaining nontrivial extensions of a \(k\)-group of multiplicative type H by a unipotent \(k\)-group U. Suppose given an action of H on U, and let E be the semidirect product \(\mathrm{E} = \mathrm{U} \cdot \mathrm{H}\). On the other hand, let there be an element of \(\mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathrm{U})\) represented by a 1-cocycle \(a\). The group U acts by inner automorphisms on E. The datum of \(a\) therefore defines a \(k\)-form of E denoted \(\mathrm{E}_a\). Suppose moreover that U is commutative; then U acts trivially on U and on the quotient \(\mathrm{E}/\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{H}\), so that \(\mathrm{E}_a\) is again an extension of H by U. Suppose, for simplicity, that H is an étale diagonalizable group, so that the Galois group \(\mathscr{G}\) of \(\bar{k}/k\) acts trivially on \(\mathrm{H}(k)\); the action of \(\mathscr{G}\) on the points of \(\mathrm{E}_a(\bar{k})\) is then given by the formula: \[ {}^g(u, h) = ({}^g u + a(g) - {}^h a(g), h) \qquad (g \in \mathscr{G}, u \in \mathrm{U}(\bar{k}), h \in \mathrm{H}(\bar{k})). \] If \(h\) is a point of \(\mathrm{H}(k)\) and X its inverse image in \(\mathrm{E}_a\), then X is a torsor under U defined by the class of the 1-cocycle of \(\mathscr{G}\) with values in U, \(g \mapsto a(g) - {}^h a(g)\). It follows that if there exists a point \(h\) of \(\mathrm{H}(k)\) such that the preceding 1-cocycle is not trivial, the extension \(\mathrm{E}_a\) is not trivial. We shall apply this construction in two particular cases:
a) Nontrivial extension of an étale diagonalizable group H by \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\).

Take for H the group \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times\), made to act by multiplication on \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\). On the other hand, let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p\), K an extension of \(k\) with Galois group \(\mathscr{G}\) isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\), and \(a\) a nonzero element of \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathscr{G}, \mathrm{U}) = \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{G}, \mathrm{U})\). The group \(\mathrm{E}_a\) then answers the question.
b) Example of a nontrivial extension of an étale diagonalizable group H by a smooth connected unipotent group U.

Take for the field \(k\) a non-perfect field such that there exists a \(k\)-form U of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) with \(\mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathrm{U}) \neq 0\). For example (cf. J.-P. Serre, Cohomologie Galoisienne), one can take for \(k\) the field of fractions of a discrete valuation ring of equal characteristic \(p > 0\), and for U the algebraic subgroup of \(\mathbb{G}_a \times_k \mathbb{G}_a\) with equation \(\mathrm{X}^p + \mathrm{X} + t\mathrm{Y}^p = 0\), where \(t\) denotes a uniformizer of \(k\). Indeed, assuming for simplicity that \(k\) contains the \((p-1)^{\text{th}}\) roots of unity, we have an exact sequence of algebraic groups: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z} \longrightarrow \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_a \longrightarrow 0, \qquad (x, y) \longmapsto y, \] hence an exact cohomology sequence: \[ \mathbb{G}_a(k) \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}) \to \mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathrm{U}) \to 0, \] where \(d\) associates with \(x\) the homogeneous space under \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) with equation \(\mathrm{X}^p + \mathrm{X} + tx^p = 0\). Moreover, it is known that \(\mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})\) is isomorphic to \(k/\mathfrak{P}(k)\) (where \(\mathfrak{P}(x) = x^p + x\)), hence \(\mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathrm{U})\) is isomorphic to \(k/(\mathfrak{P}(k) + tk^p)\). Suppose moreover \(p \neq 2\); it is then clear that \(t^2\) is an element of \(k\) not belonging to \(\mathfrak{P}(k) + tk^p\), hence \(\mathrm{H}^1(k, \mathrm{U}) \neq 0\).

On the other hand, \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{p-1}\) acts on U by the formula: \[ (h, x, y) \longmapsto (hx, hy). \] Denote by \(\mathscr{G}\) the Galois group of the extension K defined by the equation \[ \mathrm{X}^p + \mathrm{X} + t^2 = 0, \] and let \(a \in \mathrm{H}^1(\mathscr{G}, \mathrm{U})\) be the nonzero element described above. One checks immediately that \(\mathrm{E}_a\) is then a nontrivial extension of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{p-1}\) by U.
c) Nontrivial extension of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) by \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\).

By 5.1.1 i) b), such an extension can exist only over a non-perfect field \(k\). So let \(k\) be a non-perfect field, G the semidirect product of \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{G}_a\) by \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_m\), with \(\mathbb{G}_m\) acting on \(\mathbb{G}_a\) by homotheties. Since U is normal in G, \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{U}\) is normal as well. Let \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/{}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{U}\). The group \(\mathrm{G}'\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a \cdot \mathbb{G}_m\), where \(\mathbb{G}_m\) acts on \(\mathbb{G}_a\) by the formula: \[ (h, u) \longmapsto h^p u. \] The functor \(\mathscr{T}_\mathrm{G}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm{G}'}\)) of subtori of G (resp. \(\mathrm{G}'\)) (cf. Exp. XV) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\), and the morphism \(\mathscr{T}_\mathrm{G} \to \mathscr{T}_{\mathrm{G}'}\) deduced from the morphism \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) identifies with the morphism \(u \mapsto u^p\). It follows that if \(\mathrm{T}''\) is a subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}'\) corresponding to a point \(x\) of \(k \simeq \mathbb{G}_a(k)\) such that \(x^{1/p}\) is not in \(k\), the inverse image E of \(\mathrm{T}''\) in G will be an extension of a torus \(\mathrm{T}''\) by \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{U} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\), will possess no maximal tori defined over \(k\), hence will not be trivial. One finds for E the subgroup of \(\mathrm{G} = \operatorname{Spec}[\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{T}, \mathrm{T}^{-1}]\) with equation \(\mathrm{U}^p = x \mathrm{T}^p\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 5.9.1

This last example shows that a non-smooth algebraic group, defined over a non-perfect field, does not necessarily possess maximal tori, and thus answers the question raised in Exp. XIV 1.5 b).

d) Let us now give an example of a trivial extension E of a group of multiplicative type H by a unipotent group U, and of two liftings \(\mathrm{H}'\) and \(\mathrm{H}''\) of H which are not conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\).

Take for E the semidirect product of \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{G}_a\) by \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p = \operatorname{Spec} k[\mathrm{T}]/(\mathrm{T}^p - 1)\), the action of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) on \(\mathbb{G}_a = \operatorname{Spec} k[\mathrm{U}]\) being defined by the comorphism: \[ \mathrm{U} \mapsto (\mathrm{U} + \mathrm{U}^p)\mathrm{T} - \mathrm{U}^p. \] The centralizer of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) in U is then the étale group Z with equation \(\mathrm{U} + \mathrm{U}^p = 0\). It follows that if \(k\) is not algebraically closed, the canonical map \(\mathrm{U}(k) \to (\mathrm{U}/\mathrm{Z})(k)\) is not in general surjective; hence, in view of 5.1.1 ii) b), two liftings of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) into E are not necessarily conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\).

Here is another example, with \(k\) algebraically closed of characteristic \(p > 0\). Let G be the radicial group semidirect product of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) by \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\), where \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) acts on \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) by "homotheties". We then have an exact sequence of algebraic groups, with group of operators \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\): \[ 0 \to \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p \to \mathbb{G}_a \to \mathbb{G}_a \to 0, \qquad x \mapsto x^p, \] where \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) acts by homotheties on the first term \(\mathbb{G}_a\), and trivially on the second. The exact cohomology sequence (App. I, prop. 1.1) yields here the exact sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_a(k) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}^1(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p, \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}^1(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p, \mathbb{G}_a). \] Since the last term is zero (Exp. I 5.3.3), we see that \(\mathrm{H}^1(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p, \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)\) is nonzero, so two liftings of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) into G are not necessarily conjugate.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: we have replaced \(\mathrm{H}_0^i\) by \(\mathrm{H}^i\), to agree with the notation of App. I and Exp. I.
N.D.E.: This is proposition III.6.4.2 of the book by M. Demazure and P. Gabriel, Groupes algébriques I, Masson & North-Holland (1970).

pages.title: 6. Extension of a unipotent group by a group of multiplicative type

blocks.html: 6. Extension of a unipotent group by a group of multiplicative type
6.1. Statement of the theorem.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.1.1

Let \(k\) be a field, U a unipotent \(k\)-algebraic group, H a \(k\)-group of multiplicative type, E a \(k\)-algebraic group which is an extension of U by H, so that we have the exact sequence \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{E} \longrightarrow \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow 1. \] Then the extension E is trivial and there exists a unique lifting of U into E in each of the following cases:

A) The group U is smooth and one of the following conditions holds:

i) U is connected and the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{U}\) possesses a section.
ii) U possesses a composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\).
iii) H is étale.
iv) \(k\) is perfect.

B) \(\mathrm{U} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) and \(k\) is perfect.

C) E is commutative and \(k\) is perfect.
6.2. Proof of 6.1.1 A).
Let us first establish three lemmas.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.1

Let S be a prescheme, E an S-group prescheme which is an extension of an S-group prescheme U with connected fibres by an S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type H (i.e. U is the quotient of E by H for the fpqc topology). Then E is a central extension.

Indeed, since H is commutative, the group U acts by inner automorphisms on H, through an S-group morphism \[ u : \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{H}). \] The functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{H})\) is representable by an étale S-scheme (Exp. X 5.10), and consequently the unit section is an immersion that is both open and closed. Since U has connected fibres, we deduce that \(u\) is the unit morphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.2

With the notation of 6.1.1, if E is trivial, there exists a unique lifting of U into E.

So let \(\mathrm{U}'\) and \(\mathrm{U}''\) be two liftings of U into E. To show that \(\mathrm{U}' = \mathrm{U}''\), we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed, and it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H}) = 0\). If U is connected, U centralizes H (6.2.1), hence \[ \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H}) = \operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H}) = 0 \] by 2.4 ii). In the general case, denote by \(\mathrm{U}^0_1\) the unique lifting into E of the identity component \(\mathrm{U}^0\) of U, and let \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\operatorname{Norm}}_\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{U}^0_1)\). If \(g \in \mathrm{E}(k)\), \(\operatorname{int}(g)\mathrm{U}^0_1\) is a lifting of \(\mathrm{U}^0\), hence is equal to \(\mathrm{U}^0_1\), and consequently \(\mathrm{N}(k) \supset \mathrm{E}(k)\). Moreover, N dominates H (6.2.1) and \(\mathrm{U}^0_1\), whence immediately the fact that \(\mathrm{N} = \mathrm{E}\). Passing to the quotient by \(\mathrm{U}^0_1\), we are reduced to the case where U is étale. In that case, \(k\) being algebraically closed, \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H})\) identifies with the ordinary cohomology group \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{U}(k), \mathrm{H}(k))\)10 and consequently is zero, since \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) is a finite \(p\)-group and \(\mathrm{H}(k)\) is uniquely \(p\)-divisible.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2.3

To prove that E is trivial, it suffices to show that E becomes trivial after a finite separable extension of the base field; in particular, we may assume H diagonalizable.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.4

For every central extension E of U by a diagonalizable \(k\)-group H to be trivial, it suffices that every central extension of U by \(\mathbb{G}_m\) be trivial.

Indeed, by induction on \(r\), one first notes that the hypothesis made implies that E is trivial if \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_m^r\). In the general case, H embeds into \(\mathbb{G}_m^r\) for a suitable integer \(r\) (this is immediate by duality); let \(\mathrm{H}'' = \mathbb{G}_m^r/\mathrm{H}\). We obtain the exact sequence (App. I 2.1): \[ \mathrm{Z}^1(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H}'') \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{U}, \mathbb{G}_m^r) = 0 \] (where U acts trivially on H, \(\mathrm{H}''\), \(\mathbb{G}_m^r\)). But \(\mathrm{Z}^1(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H}'') = \operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H}'') = 0\) (2.4 ii)), hence \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H}) = 0\).

Proof of 6.1.1 A) i). Since \(\mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{U}\) possesses a section, E defines an element \(\overline{f}\) of \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{U}, \mathrm{H})\) (App. I 3.1). We must show that \(\overline{f}\) is zero, and for this it suffices to show that a 2-cocycle \(f : \mathrm{U} \times_k \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{H}\) is a constant morphism, which will follow from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.5

Let U be a smooth connected unipotent \(k\)-algebraic group, H a \(k\)-group of multiplicative type; then every \(k\)-morphism (of preschemes): \[ f : \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H} \] is constant.

To prove this lemma, we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed. We proceed by increasing induction on \(\dim \mathrm{U}\). If \(\dim \mathrm{U} > 0\), U possesses a composition series (cf. 3.9): \[ 1 \to \mathrm{U}' \to \mathrm{U} \xrightarrow{\ \pi\ } \mathrm{U}'' \to 1 \] with \(\mathrm{U}' \simeq \mathbb{G}_a\) and \(\dim \mathrm{U}'' < \dim \mathrm{U}\). It suffices to show that \(f\) factors through \(\mathrm{U}''\). Since the graph of the equivalence relation defined by \(\pi\) is smooth over \(k\), hence reduced, it suffices to show that if \(x, y \in \mathrm{U}(k)\) have the same image \(z\) in \(\mathrm{U}''(k)\), then \(f(x) = f(y)\). Now \(\pi^{-1}(z)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\), so the restriction of \(f\) to \(\pi^{-1}(z)\) factors through a reduced irreducible component of H, hence through a \(k\)-scheme isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_m)^r\). It then suffices to note that every morphism from \(\mathbb{G}_a\) to \((\mathbb{G}_m)^r\) is constant, since every invertible regular function on \(\mathbb{G}_a\) is constant.

Proof of 6.1.1 A) ii). Thanks to 6.2.1, 6.2.3 and 6.2.4, we may assume that \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_m\). By hypothesis, U possesses a composition series \(\mathrm{U} \supset \mathrm{U}_1 \supset \cdots\) such that \(\mathrm{U}_i/\mathrm{U}_{i+1}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\). Let \(\mathrm{E}_1\) be the inverse image of \(\mathrm{U}_1\) in E. By induction on \(\dim \mathrm{U}\), we may assume that the extension \(\mathrm{E}_1\) is trivial; let \(\mathrm{U}'_1\) be the unique lifting of \(\mathrm{U}_1\). Proceeding as in the proof of 6.2.4, one shows that \(\mathrm{U}'_1\) is normal in E. After passing to the quotient by \(\mathrm{U}'_1\), we are reduced to the case where \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{G}_a\). The S-scheme E (where \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{U}\)) is a torsor under the S-group \(\mathbb{G}_m \times_k \mathrm{S}\), hence possesses a section, since \(\operatorname{Pic}(\mathrm{S}) = 0\) (Exp. VIII 4.3). The extension E is then trivial by A) i).

Proof of 6.1.1 A) iii) (U smooth, H étale). Suppose first U connected. The group \(\mathrm{U}_{\bar{k}}\) then possesses a composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\), so \(\mathrm{E}_{\bar{k}}\) is trivial by A) ii). Since H is étale, it is clear that the unique lifting of \(\mathrm{U}_{\bar{k}}\) into \(\mathrm{E}_{\bar{k}}\) is the connected component of \(\mathrm{E}_{\bar{k}}\), so this lifting is already defined over \(k\). In the general case, after possibly passing to the quotient by the connected component of E, we are reduced to the case where U is étale, then to the case where E is completely split (6.2.3). Since \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) is a \(p\)-group and \(\mathrm{H}(k)\) has order prime to \(p\), we can take as a lifting of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) the Sylow \(p\)-subgroup of \(\mathrm{E}(k)\).

Proof of 6.1.1 A) iv) (U smooth, \(k\) perfect). If U is connected, U possesses a composition series with successive quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\) (4.1.2 b)) and we apply A) ii). In the general case, the foregoing allows us to reduce to the case where U is étale, then to the case where U is completely split and H diagonalizable (6.2.3). Now using a characteristic composition series of H (\(\mathrm{H} \supset \mathrm{H}^0 \supset {}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{H}) \supset 0\)), we reduce to the case where H is of one of the three following types:

a) H is étale.
b) \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_m^r\).
c) H is radicial.

In case a), we apply A) iii); in case c), we apply 1.6. Finally, in case b), we remark that by virtue of Hilbert's Theorem 90, \(\mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{U}\) possesses a section, so that it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{U}, \mathbb{G}_m^r) = \mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{U}(k), \mathbb{G}_m^r(k)) = 0\). Now \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) is a finite \(p\)-group, while \(\mathbb{G}_m^r(k)\) is uniquely \(p\)-divisible (since \(k\) is perfect).
6.3. Proof of 6.1.1 B) and C).

Thanks to 6.2.1, 6.2.3, 6.2.4, we see that it suffices to prove B) when \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_m\). We thus have an exact sequence: \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m \longrightarrow \mathrm{E} \longrightarrow \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p \longrightarrow 1. \] Since \(\mathbb{G}_m\) is smooth, we deduce an exact sequence of \(p\)-Lie algebras (App. II 3.2): \[ (\ast) \quad 0 \longrightarrow \operatorname{Lie} \mathbb{G}_m \longrightarrow \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Lie} \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p \longrightarrow 0. \] The group \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) being of height 1, the extension E is trivial if and only if (App. II 2.2) the exact sequence \((\ast)\) of \(p\)-Lie algebras is split. We know that \(\operatorname{Lie} \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) is generated by an element \(\mathrm{X} \neq 0\) such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = 0\) (App. II 2.1); it therefore suffices to show that X lifts to an element Z of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E}\) such that \(\mathrm{Z}^{(p)} = 0\). Now by 5.8.2 ii), there exists a unipotent element Z of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E}\) lifting X. Since the unipotent part of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E}\) is clearly of dimension at most 1, we necessarily have \(\mathrm{Z}^{(p)} = 0\).

Proof of 6.1.1 C). If \(\mathrm{U}'_1\) is a lifting into E of an algebraic subgroup \(\mathrm{U}_1\) of U, \(\mathrm{U}'_1\) is normal in E, since E is assumed commutative, and we can pass to the quotient by \(\mathrm{U}'_1\). Using a composition series of U (3.5 ii)), the preceding remark allows us to reduce to the case where U is smooth or equal to \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\). But then E is trivial by A) iv) and B).
6.4. Examples of extensions of a unipotent group U by a group of multiplicative type H which are not trivial.
In view of 6.1.1 A) iv), the problem arises only in characteristic \(p > 0\).
a) \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_m\), U a nontrivial form of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) (cf. App. III, §5).

Let \(k\) be a non-perfect field of characteristic 2, \(u\) an element of \(k\) such that \(u^{1/2}\) does not belong to \(k\). Consider the affine group E with ring \(k[\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}, (\mathrm{X}^2 + u\mathrm{Y}^2)^{-1}]\), where the multiplication is given by the comorphism: \[ (\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}) \longmapsto (\mathrm{XX}' + u\mathrm{YY}', \mathrm{XY}' + \mathrm{YX}'). \] The group E is smooth, connected, commutative, of dimension 2; the subscheme \(\mathrm{Y} = 0\) defines a subgroup \(\mathrm{H} \simeq \mathbb{G}_m\). The kernel K of squaring in E has equation: \[ \mathrm{X}^2 + u\mathrm{Y}^2 = 1, \] hence is of dimension 1. The group K contains the unipotent radical of E (defined over \(\bar{k}\)) but also the contribution of H, which is isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2\). Since K is reduced over \(k\), the unipotent radical of E is not defined over \(k\), and \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{E}/\mathrm{H}\) does not lift into E. (One checks immediately that U is the form of \(\mathbb{G}_a\) having ring \(k[\mathrm{V}, \mathrm{W}]/(\mathrm{V} + u\mathrm{V}^2 + \mathrm{W}^2)\), the morphism \(\mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{U}\) corresponding to the comorphism: \(\mathrm{V} \mapsto \mathrm{Y}^2/(\mathrm{X}^2 + \mathrm{Y}^2 u)\), \(\mathrm{W} \mapsto \mathrm{XY}/(\mathrm{X}^2 + \mathrm{Y}^2 u)\).)
b) \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_m\), \(\mathrm{U} = \mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\), \(k\) non-perfect of characteristic 2.

Choosing \(k\) and \(u\) as in a), consider the subgroup E of \(\mathrm{GL}_2\) generated by the element X such that: \[ \mathrm{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ u & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} u^{1/2} & 0 \\ 0 & u^{1/2} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & u^{-1/2} \\ u^{1/2} & 0 \end{pmatrix}. \] The group E is an extension of \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) by \(\mathbb{G}_m\), but this extension is not trivial, since the unipotent part of X is not defined over \(k\).
c) \(\mathrm{H} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), \(\mathrm{U} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\), \(k\) non-perfect.

Let \(\mathfrak{e}\) be the commutative \(p\)-Lie algebra generated by two elements X and Y such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}\) and \(\mathrm{Y}^{(p)} = a\mathrm{X}\). By App. II 2.2, \(\mathfrak{e}\) is the \(p\)-Lie algebra of an algebraic group E which is an extension of \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\) by \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), but this extension is trivial if and only if there exists \(b \in k\) such that \(b^p = a\) (for we then have \((b\mathrm{X} + \mathrm{Y})^{(p)} = 0\)).
d) \(\mathrm{H} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_2\), \(\mathrm{U} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_2 \times \boldsymbol{\alpha}_2\), E non-commutative, \(k\) a field of characteristic 2.

Consider the special linear group \(\mathrm{SL}_{2, k}\) and let \(\mathrm{E} = {}_\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{SL}_{2, k})\). The group E is a radicial group of height 1 whose Lie algebra is generated by three elements X, Y, Z satisfying the following relations: \[ [\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}] = \mathrm{Z}, \qquad [\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Z}] = [\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{Z}] = 0 \] \[ \mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{Y}^{(p)} = 0, \qquad \mathrm{Z}^{(p)} = \mathrm{Z}. \] Consequently, E is a central extension of \(\mathrm{U} \simeq \boldsymbol{\alpha}_2 \times \boldsymbol{\alpha}_2\) by \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2\). Each factor \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_2\) of U lifts in a unique way into E, but U itself does not lift into E, since \([\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}] \neq 0\).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: see, for example, proposition III.6.4.2 of the book by M. Demazure and P. Gabriel, Groupes algébriques I, Masson & North-Holland (1970).

blocks.html: 7. Nilpotent affine algebraic groups
7.1. Extensions of groups of multiplicative type.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.1.1

Let S be a prescheme, H and K two S-group preschemes of multiplicative type and of finite type, E a group prescheme which is an extension of K by H (i.e. K is the quotient of E by H for the fpqc topology). Then E is of multiplicative type in the following two cases:

a) E is commutative.

b) The fibres of K are connected.
Proof. i) Case where S is the spectrum of a field \(k\).

The assertion to be proved is local for the fpqc topology, which allows us to assume \(k\) algebraically closed, hence H and K diagonalizable. Note that K acts trivially on H by inner automorphisms: this is clear in case a), and it follows from 6.2.1 in case b). By induction on the length of a suitable composition series of K, we are reduced to the case where K is of one of the three following types: a) \(\mathrm{K} = \mathbb{G}_m\), b) \(\mathrm{K} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), c) \(\mathrm{K} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_q\) with \((q, p) = 1\) and, in that case, E is commutative. Now using an embedding of H into \(\mathbb{G}_m^r\), we deduce that E embeds into an extension of K by \(\mathbb{G}_m^r\). We may therefore assume that \(\mathrm{H} = \mathbb{G}_m^r\).
a) If \(\mathrm{K} = \mathbb{G}_m\), E is a smooth, connected, affine algebraic group (Exp. VIB 9.2) of unipotent rank zero, hence a torus.

b) \(\mathrm{K} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_p\). In this case E is a trivial extension. Indeed, since H is smooth, the canonical morphism \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{E} \to \operatorname{Lie} \boldsymbol{\mu}_p\) is surjective (App. II 3.2) and it suffices to apply 5.8.2 i), taking into account App. II 2.2.

c) \(\mathrm{K} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_q\), with \((q, p) = 1\) and E commutative. Here again the extension E is trivial. Indeed, let \(x \in \mathrm{E}(k)\) be a lifting of a generator \(\overline{x}\) of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_q(k)\). The element \(x^q\) is an element of \(\mathrm{H}(k)\), hence is of the form \(y^q\), \(y \in \mathrm{H}(k)\) (note that \(\mathbb{G}_m^r(k)\) is \(q\)-divisible). Since E is commutative, \(y^{-1}x\) is a lifting of \(x\) which is of order \(q\).

ii) General case. The groups H and K are flat, affine and of finite presentation over S (Exp. IX 2.1) and consequently so is E (Exp. VIB 9.2). Using then the general technique of VIB §10, we reduce to the case where S is noetherian. To show that E is of multiplicative type, it then suffices to prove that E is commutative and that \({}_n\mathrm{E}\) is finite over S for every \(n\) (Exp. X 4.8 b)).

a) E is commutative. We must check that the morphism: \[ \mathrm{E} \times_\mathrm{S} \mathrm{E} \longrightarrow \mathrm{E}, \qquad (x, y) \mapsto [x, y] = xyx^{-1}y^{-1} \] factors through the unit section of E, and it suffices to check this when S is the spectrum of an artinian local ring. But then E is of multiplicative type by i) and Exp. X 2.3.

b) \({}_n\mathrm{E}\) is finite over S. Indeed, we have the exact sequence: \[ 0 \to {}_n\mathrm{H} \to {}_n\mathrm{E} \to {}_n\mathrm{K} \xrightarrow{\ u\ } \mathrm{H}_n \] (where \(\mathrm{H}_n\) is the cokernel of raising to the \(n^{\text{th}}\) power in H). We know that \(\mathrm{H}_n\) is of multiplicative type (Exp. IX 2.7), hence separated, and that \({}_n\mathrm{H}\) and \({}_n\mathrm{K}\) are finite over S; \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) is a closed subgroup of \({}_n\mathrm{K}\), hence is finite over S, and \({}_n\mathrm{E}\) is finite over S, being an extension of a finite group by a finite group (Exp. VIB 9.2).
7.2. Structure of commutative affine algebraic groups.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.2.1

Let \(k\) be a field, G a commutative affine \(k\)-algebraic group. Then:

a) G contains a largest subgroup of multiplicative type M. The group M is characteristic in G and \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{M}\) is unipotent, and its formation commutes with extension of the field \(k\).

b) If \(k\) is perfect, G is the direct product of M and a unipotent algebraic subgroup U, and this in a unique way.
Proof. i) \(k\) algebraically closed.

When G is smooth, 7.2.1 b) is well known (BIBLE §4 Th. 4). If G is radicial of height 1, to the decomposition of \(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}\) described in 4.2.2 corresponds, in view of 4.3.1 v) and App. II 2.2, a decomposition of G of the type 7.2.1 b). In the general case, G admits a composition series whose successive quotients are smooth or radicial of height 1 (App. II 3.1). To prove 7.2.1 b), it then suffices to note that if we have an exact sequence of commutative algebraic groups: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}'' \longrightarrow 0, \] where \(\mathrm{G}'\) (resp. \(\mathrm{G}''\)) is the product of a group of multiplicative type and a unipotent group, \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{M}' \cdot \mathrm{U}'\) (resp. \(\mathrm{G}'' = \mathrm{M}'' \cdot \mathrm{U}''\)), then the same holds for G. Indeed, consider the exact sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}'/\mathrm{U}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{U}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}'' \longrightarrow 0. \] By 7.1.1 a), the inverse image in \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{U}'\) of \(\mathrm{M}''\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{M}_1\). The group \(\mathrm{M}_1\) lifts to a subgroup M of G (5.1.1 i) a)). Likewise, using this time 6.1.1 C), one proves that there exists a unipotent subgroup U of G which is an extension of \(\mathrm{U}''\) by \(\mathrm{U}'\). It is clear that \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{M} \cdot \mathrm{U}\).

ii) \(k\) arbitrary. By i), \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) is the direct product of a group of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{M}_{\bar{k}}\) and a unipotent group \(\mathrm{U}_{\bar{k}}\). Set \(\mathrm{S} = \bar{k} \times_k \bar{k}\) and let \(\mathrm{M}_1\) and \(\mathrm{M}_2\) be the two inverse images of \(\mathrm{M}_{\bar{k}}\) under the two projections \(\mathrm{S} \rightrightarrows \bar{k}\). The group \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}/\mathrm{M}_2 = (\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{M}_{\bar{k}})_\mathrm{S}\) has unipotent fibres, so the image of \(\mathrm{M}_1\) in \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}/\mathrm{M}_2\) is zero (2.4 ii)) and \(\mathrm{M}_2\) dominates \(\mathrm{M}_1\). Likewise \(\mathrm{M}_1\) dominates \(\mathrm{M}_2\), and finally \(\mathrm{M}_1 = \mathrm{M}_2\). By fpqc descent, it follows that \(\mathrm{M}_{\bar{k}}\) comes from an algebraic subgroup M of G. It is clear that M is of multiplicative type, that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{M}\) is unipotent, and that the formation of M is compatible with any extension of the field \(k\). For every \(k\)-prescheme S, every subgroup of multiplicative type H of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}\) is contained in \(\mathrm{M}_\mathrm{S}\). Indeed, by 2.5, its image in the group with unipotent fibres \((\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{M})_\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}/\mathrm{M}_\mathrm{S}\) is zero. Taking in particular for H the transform of \(\mathrm{M}_\mathrm{S}\) by an automorphism of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}\), we deduce that M is characteristic in G.
Finally, if \(k\) is perfect, \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{M}\) lifts into G to a unipotent group, and this in a unique way by 6.1.1 C).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 7.2.2

i) If \(k\) is not perfect, the unipotent component of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) is not necessarily defined over \(k\), as example 6.4 a) shows.

ii) Contrary to what happens for the multiplicative-type component M, the unipotent component U is not in general characteristic in G (and this whatever the characteristic of \(k\)). Of course, the uniqueness of the decomposition 7.2.1 b) implies that U is invariant under every \(k\)-automorphism of G. But if U and M are such that there exist a \(k\)-prescheme S and a nonzero S-homomorphism \(h : \mathrm{U}_\mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{M}_\mathrm{S}\) (cf. 2.6), we deduce an S-automorphism of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S}\), \((u, m) \mapsto (u, h(u) + m)\), which does not leave \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{S}\) invariant.

iii) If G is finite over \(k\), \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{M}\) corresponds by Cartier duality (2.6) to the connected component of the dual \(\mathrm{D}(\mathrm{G})\) of G.
7.3. Structure of nilpotent affine algebraic groups.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.3.1

Let \(k\) be a field, G a nilpotent (Exp. VIB §8), affine, connected \(k\)-algebraic group. Then G possesses a largest subgroup of multiplicative type M. The group M is central and characteristic, and \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{M}\) is a unipotent algebraic group.

Let Z be the centre of G, M the largest subgroup of multiplicative type of Z (7.2.1). Since Z is characteristic in G and M characteristic in Z, M is characteristic in G. It suffices to show that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{M}\) is unipotent. By induction on the length of the ascending central series of G, this will follow more generally from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3.2

(Rosenlicht). Let G be a connected \(k\)-algebraic group, Z its centre. Then the centre \(\mathrm{Z}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is unipotent.

Proof. We may assume \(k\) algebraically closed. It then suffices to show that \(\mathrm{Z}'\) contains no subgroup isomorphic to \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\ell\) for any prime number \(\ell\) (4.6.1 vi)). So let \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\ell\) be a subgroup of \(\mathrm{Z}'\), N its inverse image in G. Since \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\ell\) is central in \(\mathrm{G}'\), N is normal in G.
i) Case where \((\ell, p) = 1\). One can find an element \(x\) of \(\mathrm{N}(k)\) and an integer \(n\) with the following properties:

a) \(x\) lifts a generator \(\overline{x}\) of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\ell\).
b) \(x^{\ell^n} \in \mathrm{Z}^0(k)\);
(it suffices to choose a lifting of \(\overline{x}\) whose image in \(\mathrm{N}/\mathrm{Z}^0(k)\) belongs to the Sylow \(\ell\)-subgroup).

Raising to the \(\ell^{\text{th}}\) power in the commutative group \(\mathrm{Z}^0\) is an étale morphism, so \(\mathrm{Z}^0(k)\) is \(\ell\)-divisible. Consequently, after possibly multiplying \(x\) by an element of \(\mathrm{Z}^0(k)\), we may assume that \(x^{\ell^n} = 0\). The group N is then generated by two commutative groups which commute with each other (Z and the group generated by \(x\)), hence is commutative. The group \({}_{\ell^n} \mathrm{N}\) is a group of multiplicative type, characteristic in N, hence normal in G, and consequently central, G being connected (Exp. IX 5.5). Hence \({}_{\ell^n} \mathrm{N}\) is contained in Z, which contradicts the fact that its image in \(\mathrm{G}'\) contains \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_\ell\).

ii) \(\ell = p\). There then exists an integer \(n\) such that the image of \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n} \mathrm{N}\) in \(\mathrm{G}'\) contains \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), so that we have the exact sequence: \[ (\ast) \quad 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{K} \longrightarrow {}_{\mathrm{F}^n} \mathrm{N} \longrightarrow \boldsymbol{\mu}_p \longrightarrow 1. \] The group K is contained in Z, hence is commutative; it then follows from 7.2.1 and 7.1.1 b) that there exists a subgroup of multiplicative type contained in \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n} \mathrm{N}\) whose image in the quotient by K is \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\). We deduce as in i) that \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n} \mathrm{N}\) is commutative. The multiplicative-type component of \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n} \mathrm{N}\) (7.2.1) is characteristic in \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n} \mathrm{N}\), hence normal in G, hence central (Exp. IX 5.5); since its image in \(\mathrm{G}'\) contains \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\), we obtain a contradiction.

pages.title: Appendix I. Hochschild cohomology and extensions of algebraic groups

blocks.html: Appendix I. Hochschild cohomology and extensions of algebraic groups
A.1. Definition of the cohomology groups.

Let \(k\) be a field, G a \(k\)-algebraic group, \((\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\) the abelian category of commutative \(k\)-algebraic groups on which G acts. If \(\mathrm{A} \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\), the functor \(h_\mathrm{A} : (\mathbf{Sch}/k)^\circ \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) canonically defined by A is a G-\(\mathbb{Z}\)-module in the sense of I 3.2. We can therefore consider the standard complex \(\mathrm{C}^\bullet(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) of algebraic cochains of G with values in A (Exp. I 5.1), as well as the group of \(i\)-cocycles \(\mathrm{Z}^i(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\), of \(i\)-coboundaries \(\mathrm{B}^i(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\), and the \(i^{\text{th}}\) cohomology group \(\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\). As usual, \(\mathrm{H}^0(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) identifies with the group \(\mathrm{A}^\mathrm{G}(k)\), where \(\mathrm{A}^\mathrm{G}\) is the \(k\)-functor of invariants of A under G. The group \(\mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) classifies the trivial principal homogeneous spaces under A on which G acts.

The functor \(\mathrm{A} \mapsto \mathrm{H}^\bullet(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) is not in general a cohomological functor from the category \((\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\) with values in \(\mathbf{Ab}\); however, we have the following proposition:

blocks.label: A.1.1
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition A.1.1

Let \(0 \xrightarrow{u} \mathrm{A}' \xrightarrow{v} \mathrm{A} \to \mathrm{A}'' \to 0\) be an exact sequence in \((\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\). Then:

a) If \(v\) possesses a section (that is, if there exists a \(k\)-morphism of preschemes \(s : \mathrm{A}'' \to \mathrm{A}\) such that \(vs = 1_{\mathrm{A}''}\)), we have the usual exact cohomology sequence: \[ \cdots \to \mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \to \mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}'') \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \mathrm{H}^{i+1}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}') \to \cdots \]

b) If \(\mathrm{A}(k) \to \mathrm{A}''(k)\) is surjective, we have the exact sequence: \[ (1) \quad 0 \to \mathrm{A}'^\mathrm{G}(k) \to \mathrm{A}^\mathrm{G}(k) \to \mathrm{A}''^\mathrm{G}(k) \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}') \to \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \to \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}''). \]

Proof. a) One notes that the existence of a section implies the exactness of the sequence of complexes: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}^\bullet(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}') \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}^\bullet(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}^\bullet(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}'') \longrightarrow 0. \]

b) If \(x'' \in \mathrm{A}''^\mathrm{G}(k)\), its inverse image in A is a principal homogeneous space under \(\mathrm{A}'\), trivial (since \(\mathrm{A}(k) \to \mathrm{A}''(k)\) is assumed surjective), on which G acts, hence defines an element \(d(x'') \in \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}')\). The exactness of the sequence \((1)\) is then immediate.
A.2. The group \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\).

Let A and G be two \(k\)-algebraic groups, E and \(\mathrm{E}'\) two \(k\)-algebraic groups which are extensions of G by A. These two extensions are said to be isomorphic if there exists a \(k\)-group morphism \(u : \mathrm{E} \to \mathrm{E}'\) making the following diagram commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathrm{E} \ar[dd]^u \ar[dr] & \\ \mathrm{A} \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & & \mathrm{G} \\ & \mathrm{E}' \ar[ur] & } \] The group E acts upon A by inner automorphisms, and if A is commutative, this action factors through G, so that G acts upon A. Conversely, if \(\mathrm{A} \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\), we denote by \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) the set of classes of algebraic extensions E of G by A for which the action of G upon A defined by E and the action coming from the structure of object of \((\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\) coincide.
\(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) is in a natural way a bifunctor, covariant in A and contravariant in G. More precisely:

a) if \(f : \mathrm{A} \to \mathrm{B}\) is a morphism in \((\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\), and if E represents an element of \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\), one defines \(f_*(\mathrm{E}) \in \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{B})\) as the class of the extension of G by B equal to the quotient of the semidirect product \(\mathrm{B} \cdot \mathrm{E}\) (E acting on B through G) by the algebraic subgroup image of A under the morphism \((f, i)\) (this quotient is representable by Exp. VIA §5), so that we have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{A} \ar[r]^i \ar[d]_f & \mathrm{E} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{G} \ar[r] \ar[d]^{1_\mathrm{G}} & 1 \\ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{B} \ar[r] & f_*(\mathrm{E}) \ar[r] & \mathrm{G} \ar[r] & 1. } \]

b) If \(g : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) is a \(k\)-morphism of \(k\)-algebraic groups, and if E is an extension of G by A, the fibre product \(\mathrm{E} \times_\mathrm{G} \mathrm{H}\) is in a natural way an extension of H by A, denoted \(g^*(\mathrm{E})\). We have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{A} \ar[r] \ar[d]_{1_\mathrm{A}} & g^*(\mathrm{E}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{H} \ar[r] \ar[d]^g & 1 \\ 1 \ar[r] & \mathrm{A} \ar[r] & \mathrm{E} \ar[r] & \mathrm{G} \ar[r] & 1. } \]

Adapting the proofs given in J.-P. Serre, Groupes algébriques et corps de classe, chap. VII, one equips \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) with a natural structure of abelian group, functorial in A and G.

blocks.label: A.2.1
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition A.2.1

Let \(0 \to \mathrm{A}' \to \mathrm{A} \to \mathrm{A}'' \to 0\) be an exact sequence in \((\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\); then:

a) We have a canonical exact sequence of abelian groups: \[ \mathrm{Z}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \to \mathrm{Z}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}'') \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}') \to \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \to \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}''). \]

b) If \(\mathrm{A}(k) \to \mathrm{A}''(k)\) is surjective, we deduce from a) the exact sequence: \[ (2) \quad \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \to \mathrm{H}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}'') \xrightarrow{\ d\ } \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}') \to \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \to \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}''). \]

The exact sequence of a) generalizes the usual exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Hom}(\cdot, \cdot)\) valid in the setting of commutative extensions (loc. cit.) and is proved in the same way. Let us simply recall the definition of the coboundary \(d : \mathrm{Z}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}'') \to \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}')\). To this end, consider the extension \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{A}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathrm{A}'' \cdot \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow 1, \] deduced in the obvious way from the exact sequence \(0 \to \mathrm{A}' \to \mathrm{A} \to \mathrm{A}'' \to 0\). If \(u \in \mathrm{Z}^1(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}'')\), \(u\) defines in the usual way a section homomorphism \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{A}'' \cdot \mathrm{G}\). We then have \(d(u) = u^*(\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{G})\).
A.3. Comparison of \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) and \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\).

It is well known, in the case of abstract groups, that there exists a functorial isomorphism between the abelian groups \(\mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) and \(\operatorname{Ext}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\). Likewise in the present case, if A is an element of \((\mathbf{Ab})_\mathrm{G}\), to every 2-cocycle \(u \in \mathrm{Z}^2(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) one can associate a structure of algebraic group on the prescheme \(\mathrm{A} \times_k \mathrm{G}\) making it an element of \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\). Moreover this extension is trivial if and only if \(u \in \mathrm{B}^2(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) (cf. Exp. III 1.2.2). Recall that the composition law on \(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{G}\) is defined by the formula: \[ (a, g)(a', g') = a + {}^g a' + u(g, g'). \] It is clear that the extensions of G by A thus obtained are not arbitrary, since they possess a section. But conversely, if \(\mathrm{E} \in \operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) possesses a section \(s\), E is isomorphic to the extension of G by A associated with the 2-cocycle \(u\) such that: \[ u(g, g') = s(g)s(g')s(gg')^{-1}. \] We finally obtain the following proposition:

blocks.label: A.3.1
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition A.3.1

There exists a functorial isomorphism between the bifunctors with values in abelian groups: \[ (\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \mapsto \mathrm{H}^2(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \qquad \text{and} \qquad (\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}) \mapsto \operatorname{Ext}_s(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A}), \] where \(\operatorname{Ext}_s(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) denotes the subgroup of \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\text{alg}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{A})\) consisting of the classes of extensions of G by A which possess a section.

pages.title: Appendix II. Reminders and complements on radicial groups

blocks.html: Appendix II. Reminders and complements on radicial groups
Let \(p\) be a prime number \(> 1\) and let S be an \(\mathbb{F}_p\)-prescheme.
B.1. The Frobenius morphism.

For every S-prescheme X and every integer \(n > 0\), denote by \(\mathrm{P}^n : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{X}\) the \(\mathbb{F}_p\)-endomorphism corresponding to raising to the \(p^n\)-th power in \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{X}\), and denote by \(\mathrm{X}^{(n)}\) the S-prescheme inverse image of X under the morphism \(\mathrm{P}^n : \mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{S}\).
