There then exists a unique S-morphism \(\mathrm{F}^n : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{X}^{(n)}\) making the following diagram commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathrm{X} \ar[dl]_{\mathrm{P}^n} \ar[dr]^{\mathrm{F}^n} & \\ \mathrm{X} \ar[d] & & \mathrm{X}^{(n)} \ar[ll] \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{S} & & \mathrm{S} \ar[ll]^{\mathrm{P}^n} } \] It is clear that \(\mathrm{F}^n\) identifies with the "\(n^{\text{th}}\) iterate" of \(\mathrm{F}^1 = \mathrm{F}\), called the Frobenius morphism of \(\mathrm{X}/\mathrm{S}\).

If G is an S-group prescheme, \(\mathrm{G}^{(n)}\) is an S-group prescheme and \(\mathrm{F}^n : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}^{(n)}\) is an S-group morphism. Its kernel \({}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G})\) is a characteristic sub-group prescheme of G (i.e. stable under the functor \(\underline{\operatorname{Aut}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{G})\)), and radicial over S. If G is a radicial S-group prescheme, we say that G is of height \(\leqslant h\) if \({}_{\mathrm{F}^h}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{G}\).
B.2. Groups and \(p\)-Lie algebras.

If G is an S-group prescheme, \(\operatorname{Lie}(\mathrm{G})\) (Exp. II) is in a natural way a restricted \(p\)-Lie Algebra (Exp. VIIA §§5 and 6). In particular we have the following result (cf. Exp. VIIA):

blocks.label: B.2.1
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition B.2.1

i) \(\operatorname{Lie}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p)_\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Lie}(\mathbb{G}_a)_\mathrm{S}\)11 is a sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-modules, free over \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) of rank 1, generated by an element X such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = 0\).

ii) \(\operatorname{Lie}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_p)_\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Lie}(\mathbb{G}_m)_\mathrm{S}\) is a sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\)-modules, free over \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\) of rank 1, possessing a canonical basis X such that \(\mathrm{X}^{(p)} = \mathrm{X}\).
Let us now recall the fundamental result proved in Exp. VIIA §7:

blocks.label: B.2.2
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem B.2.2

Suppose S affine with ring A. Then the functor: \[ \mathrm{G} \longmapsto \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G} \] establishes an equivalence of categories between, on the one hand, the category of S-group preschemas G of finite presentation and flat over S, of height 1, whose Lie algebra is locally free over \(\mathscr{O}_\mathrm{S}\), and, on the other hand, the category of restricted \(p\)-A-Lie algebras which are locally free of finite rank.

Moreover, if G is as above and if H is an S-group prescheme of finite presentation, the canonical morphism: \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{H}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{p\text{-A-Lie}}(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}), \operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S})) \] is an isomorphism.
B.3. Radicial groups and smooth groups.
We now assume that S is the spectrum of a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p\).

Recall that in Exp. VIA §5 it was shown that if G is a \(k\)-algebraic group and H an algebraic subgroup of G, then the sheaf \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) (sheaf for the fpqc topology) is representable. Recall then (VIIA 8.3):

blocks.label: B.3.1
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition B.3.1

Let G be a \(k\)-algebraic group. Then there exists an integer \(m\) such that for every \(n \geqslant m\), the algebraic group \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{F}^n(\mathrm{G})\) is smooth over \(k\).

blocks.label: B.3.2
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition B.3.2

Consider an exact sequence of \(k\)-algebraic groups: \[ 1 \to \mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{G} \xrightarrow{\ u\ } \mathrm{G}'' \to 1 \] and the following assertions:

i) The morphism \(u\) is smooth.

ii) \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth over \(k\).

iii) For every integer \(n > 0\), we have the exact sequence: \[ 1 \longrightarrow {}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G}') \longrightarrow {}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G}) \longrightarrow {}_{\mathrm{F}^n}(\mathrm{G}'') \longrightarrow 1. \]

iv) The morphism \({}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G} \to {}_\mathrm{F} \mathrm{G}''\) is an epimorphism.

v) The morphism \((\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G})(k) \to (\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}'')(k)\) is surjective.

Then we have the following implications: \[ \mathrm{i}) \Longleftrightarrow \mathrm{ii}) \Longrightarrow \mathrm{iii}) \Longrightarrow \mathrm{iv}) \Longleftrightarrow (\mathrm{v}). \] Moreover, if G is smooth, the five assertions are equivalent.
i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) ii) by Exp. VIB 9.2 vii).
ii) \(\Rightarrow\) iii). If \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth, \(\mathrm{F}^n : \mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{G}'^{(n)}\) is an epimorphism and iii) follows from the "snake" diagram.
iii) \(\Rightarrow\) iv) is clear.
iv) \(\Leftrightarrow\) v) by theorem B.2.2.

v) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) when G is smooth. Indeed \(\mathrm{G}''\) is then smooth, and v) implies that we have \(\dim \mathrm{G}' = \dim_k(\operatorname{Lie} \mathrm{G}')(k)\), so \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: we have removed here the definition of \(\boldsymbol{\alpha}_p\), already given at the beginning of this Exposé.

pages.title: Appendix III. Remarks and complements concerning exposés XV, XVI, XVII

blocks.html: Appendix III. Remarks and complements concerning exposés XV, XVI, XVII
C.1.

It may be that propositions 1.2 and 1.2 bis of XV remain true if one removes the hypothesis that \(\mathrm{H}_0\) is smooth over \(\mathrm{S}_0\). This is in particular the case if G is finite, flat and commutative.
C.2. Complement to XV 4.8.
The following proposition, as well as theorems C.3.1 and C.4.1 below, will appear in an article in preparation by M. Raynaud on group schemes over a discrete valuation ring.12

blocks.label: C.2.1
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition C.2.1

Let S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, \(t\) its generic point, G an S-group prescheme of finite type and flat, \(\widetilde{\mathrm{G}} = \operatorname{Spec} \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_\mathrm{G})\), \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) the canonical morphism. Then:

(1) \(\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) is in a natural way an S-group scheme and \(u\) is a homomorphism.

(2) \(\operatorname{Ker}(u)\) is flat over S and \((\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}, u)\) is an fpqc quotient of G by \(\operatorname{Ker}(u)\), so that \(\widetilde{\mathrm{G}}\) is the largest affine quotient of G.

(3) If \(\mathrm{G}_t\) is affine, \(\operatorname{Ker}(u)\) is a group étale over S, equal to the unit group if and only if G is separated over S. In particular, an S-group scheme G, flat, of finite type, separated, with affine generic fibre is affine.
C.3.

In the statement of XV 6.6, the hypothesis that H be equal to its connected normalizer is unnecessary for the set of points \(s\) of S such that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a parabolic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) to be an open set. Indeed, let us take up again the proof given in paragraph c) preceding lemma 6.9, and denote by \(\overline{\mathrm{N}}\) the schematic closure of \(\mathrm{N}_t\) in G. Thus \(\overline{\mathrm{N}}\) is a closed sub-group scheme of G, flat over S, which dominates H and is contained in N. It then follows from the theorem below that \(\mathrm{G}/\overline{\mathrm{N}}\) is representable. Since \(\mathrm{G}_s/\overline{\mathrm{N}}_s\) is proper and connected, we finish as in loc. cit.

blocks.label: C.3.1
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem C.3.1

13 Let S be the spectrum of a henselian discrete valuation ring, G an S-group prescheme locally of finite type, H a closed sub-group prescheme of G, flat over S; then \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is representable.
C.4. Complement to (XVI 1.1).
The following theorem makes (VIII 7.9) more precise.

blocks.label: C.4.1
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem C.4.1

Let S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring of residual characteristic \(p > 0\) and let G be an S-group scheme, commutative, smooth, of finite type and separated over S. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \({}_p\mathrm{G}\) is finite over S.

(ii) For every S-prescheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) and for every \(\mathrm{S}'\)-group prescheme \(\mathrm{H}'\), of finite presentation over \(\mathrm{S}'\), separated over \(\mathrm{S}'\), every \(\mathrm{S}'\)-monomorphism \(u : \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'} \to \mathrm{H}'\) is an immersion.
C.5.

Example (XVII 6.4 a)) provides an example of a smooth group over a field \(k\) whose unipotent radical is not defined over \(k\). The following proposition gives a general method for obtaining such groups:

blocks.label: C.5.1
blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition C.5.1

Let \(k\) be a field, K a finite radicial extension of \(k\) of degree \(> 1\), H a connected, smooth K-algebraic group of dimension \(r\), \(\mathrm{G} = \prod_{\mathrm{K}/k} \mathrm{H}/\mathrm{K}\), which is a smooth, connected \(k\)-algebraic group of dimension \(r[\mathrm{K} : k]\). Let \(u : \mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K} \to \mathrm{H}\) be the canonical homomorphism, and \(\mathrm{R} = \operatorname{Ker}(u)\). Then:

i) The morphism \(u\) is an epimorphism and R is a smooth, unipotent, connected algebraic group.

ii) If U is a smooth algebraic subgroup of G such that \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) dominates R, then \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{G}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary

Keep the notation of the preceding proposition.

a) If H is not unipotent, the unipotent radical of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) is not defined over \(k\).

b) If H is a nonzero abelian variety, G is not an extension of an abelian variety by a smooth linear group.

c) If H is not solvable, the solvable radical of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar{k}}\) is not defined over \(k\), and G possesses no Borel subgroup defined over \(k\).
Let us first show how the corollary follows from the proposition.

a) Let U be a unipotent radical of G. Then \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) is the unipotent radical of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}\), hence dominates R, since R is smooth unipotent connected by i), so \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{G}\) by ii). But \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}\) admits H as a quotient, and H is not unipotent by hypothesis, so \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}\) is not unipotent, whence a contradiction.

b) If G is an extension of an abelian variety by a linear group L, smooth over \(k\), necessarily \(\mathrm{L}_\mathrm{K}\) dominates R, hence \(\mathrm{L} = \mathrm{G}\). But G cannot be a linear group, since \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}\) possesses a quotient which is a nonzero abelian variety.

c) Let S (resp. B) be a solvable radical of G (resp. a Borel subgroup of G); then \(\mathrm{S}_\mathrm{K}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{B}_\mathrm{K}\)) dominates R, hence \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{G}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{B} = \mathrm{G}\)). But then G is solvable, which contradicts the fact that \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}\) possesses a quotient which is not solvable.
Proof of proposition 5.1:

i) Let us begin by describing the morphism \(u\). The datum of a K-scheme \[ f : \mathrm{S} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{K}) \] allows us to construct the diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{K}) \ar[d]_j & \mathrm{T} \ar[l]_h \ar[d]^{j_\mathrm{T}} \\ \operatorname{Spec}(k) & \mathrm{S} \ar[l]^g \ar[ul]^(0.35)f \ar@/^1.2ex/[u]^s } \] where \(g = j \circ f\), \(\mathrm{T} = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{K}) \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(k)} \mathrm{S}\), \(h\) and \(j_\mathrm{T}\) are the two projections, and \(s\) is the section of T over S such that \(h \circ s = f\). The map \[ u(\mathrm{S}) : \mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{S}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S}) \] is simply the composite map: \[ \mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{S}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{T}) \to \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S}), \] where the last arrow is defined by the section \(s\).

Take in particular for S the spectrum of an algebraic closure \(\bar{k}\) of \(k\) and for \(f\) the unique \(k\)-morphism \(\mathrm{K} \to \bar{k}\), so that T is a local artinian scheme. To prove i) it suffices to do so after the extension \(\mathrm{K} \to \bar{k}\) of the base field. Now it is clear that \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{S} = \prod_{\mathrm{T}/\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{H}_\mathrm{T}/\mathrm{T}\) represents the Greenberg functor of \(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{T}\) relative to S (M. J. Greenberg, Schemata over local rings, Ann. of Maths. 73, 1961, p. 624-648). The description made above then shows that, via this last identification, \(u_\mathrm{S}\) is the canonical transition morphism: \[ \operatorname{Green}(\mathrm{H}_\mathrm{T}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{H}_\mathrm{T} \times_\mathrm{T} \mathrm{S}. \] Assertion i) then follows from the fact that H is smooth over K and from (M. J. Greenberg, Schemata over local rings II, Ann. of Maths. 78, 1963, p. 256-266).

ii) To establish ii) we may assume \(k\) separably closed. So let U be a smooth algebraic subgroup of G such that \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K} \supset \mathrm{R}\), and let us show that \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{G}\). Since G is connected, we may assume U connected. Let V be the smooth connected algebraic subgroup \(u(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K})\) of H and \(\mathrm{V}' = \prod_{\mathrm{K}/k} \mathrm{V}/\mathrm{K}\), which is a smooth connected algebraic subgroup of G. The group \(\mathrm{V}'_\mathrm{K}\) is an algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_\mathrm{K}\) and the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{V}'_\mathrm{K} \to \mathrm{V}\) is simply the restriction of \(u\) to \(\mathrm{V}'_\mathrm{K}\). By hypothesis, \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) dominates R; a fortiori, \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) dominates \(\operatorname{Ker}(\mathrm{V}'_\mathrm{K} \to \mathrm{V})\); on the other hand, by construction, the image of \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) in H is equal to V. We deduce that \(\mathrm{U}_\mathrm{K}\) dominates \(\mathrm{V}'_\mathrm{K}\), hence that U dominates \(\mathrm{V}'\). On the other hand, the canonical isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{G}(k) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{K}) \xrightarrow{\ u(\mathrm{K})\ } \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{K}) \] obviously sends \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) into \(\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{K}) = \mathrm{V}'(k)\); this says that \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) is contained in \(\mathrm{V}'(k)\). Since U is smooth and \(k\) separably closed, this implies \(\mathrm{U} \subset \mathrm{V}'\), whence \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{V}'\). We then have the equalities: \[ ([\mathrm{K} : k] - 1) \dim \mathrm{V} = \dim \operatorname{Ker}(\mathrm{V}'_\mathrm{K} \to \mathrm{V}) = \dim \mathrm{R} = ([\mathrm{K} : k] - 1) \dim \mathrm{H}. \] Since \(\mathrm{K} \neq k\), we conclude \(\dim \mathrm{V} = \dim \mathrm{H}\), whence \(\mathrm{V} = \mathrm{H}\) and finally \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{V}' = \mathrm{G}\).
